data:
Draft · the whole Confirmation curriculum is still being polished 9th Grade. The entry point into full participation in the life of the church.
Let’s be very clear about what Confirmation is — and is not:
It is not graduation from church. It is not a gold star for attending. It is not a hoop to jump through so parents get the photo. It is three things, all at once:
God confirms a youth’s baptismal identity as a beloved child of God. The youth confirms their faith in Jesus Christ — not their parents’ faith, not their grandmother’s faith, their own. Everyone confirms that this is the beginning, not the end. Confirmation works best when it’s relational, not informational. A student will forget most of the doctrine by Thanksgiving. They won’t forget the adult who knew their name, asked their hard questions, and prayed for them by name on Confirmation Sunday.
data.how-this-section-is-organized#How this s:
A Confirmation year is more than a stack of lessons — it’s a program with a calendar, a roster, a rhythm of parent communication, two big events, and a student project that lands on Confirmation Sunday. This section is split accordingly:
SessionsThe twelve-topic curriculum — Covenant to Crossing Over draft Running the YearSample calendar, roster, attendance, group chat, leader + mentor guides draft CommunicationsInvite templates and the season-long parent email series draft EventsConfirmation Dessert and the Children & Youth Sunday service draft Statement of FaithThe credo project: framework, guides, and student examples draft
data.the-arc-at-a-glance#The arc, at a glance:
Twelve topics, loosely anchored to the Acts 2:42 “devote yourselves to…” vows in the last four:
Covenant — what God has always been up to Trust — knowing and trusting God Sin — naming what’s broken Lord — Jesus as Lord, not just teacher Salvation — who is your Savior? Disciple — following, not just admiring Love — the shape of Christ’s love Teaching — “will you devote yourself to the apostles’ teaching?” Fellowship — "…and to the fellowship?" Breaking Bread — "…and to the breaking of bread?" Prayer — "…and to the prayers?" Crossing Over — writing a statement of faith for Confirmation Sunday

Draft · still being polished Every template you’ll send in a year of Confirmation.
A full Confirmation year involves a lot of writing to parents and students — more than most directors realize when they start. This section collects every message template we use, in the order we send it.
InvitesEmail + mailing templates for recruiting next year’s class draft Parent EmailsThe season-long email series that runs alongside each session draft Every template is editable markdown. Copy, paste into your email client, and adjust names and dates.

Draft · still being polished Recruiting next year’s class.
These go out in the spring — usually to the rising 9th-grade confirmation list (current 8th-graders). We send both: an email invite for families who are already in the digital loop, and a physical mailing for families who aren’t. Both should arrive the same week.
The mailing includes the same content as the email, but with a little more warmth and a printed calendar preview on the back. Parents save paper; kids don’t read email.

The email we send to invite rising 9th graders.
Replace the bracketed bits with your church name, dates, leader names, and registration link.
Subject: Confirmation Class at [Church]!
Dear Student and Parent/Guardian,
We are delighted to invite you to be a part of the 2025–2026 Confirmation class at [Church Name]!
Confirmation class is an opportunity for students to strengthen their faith and learn what it means to follow Jesus in the real world. It culminates in an opportunity to make a public profession of faith and become a member of [Church Name].
Parents are expected to play an active role during their child’s Confirmation journey. This includes regularly attending church, attending select classes, participating in conversations about life and faith with their children, and engaging in spiritual practices with their families.
Confirmation class is designed for students in 9th grade or above and it will meet on Sundays from 9:45–10:45am at [location]. Come early for bagels. The first class kicks off on October 5th.
Students are expected to commit to attending all classes and activities. The year includes ~24 Sunday morning classes and a few extra events. We do understand that some absences are unavoidable.
Please prayerfully consider your decision to join Confirmation and feel free to reach out with any questions or concerns. If you decide to attend, please fill out the registration form linked below.
We are excited at the possibility of journeying with you through the next year.
In Christ, The Confirmation Teachers [Leader names]
Click HERE for the registration form. ([link])

data.cadence#Cadence: Per-session emails (12 total, matching the twelve topics) Event emails (parent class, dessert, Confirmation Sunday) Seasonal touchpoints (post-fall-retreat, end-of-year) These are templates. Keep the voice, swap the specifics. The value of the series is the rhythm — parents know something’s landing in their inbox every couple of weeks, so they lean in instead of tuning out.

data:
Sent after Session 1B Covenant.
Attachments:
Annual calendar For Me and My House — family-covenant worksheet Hello Confirmation Parents,
Attached is our calendar for the year and a sheet for an activity explained below.
Aside from the parent class dates, please also mark your calendars to join us for 3/21 (Serve Saturday), 5/28 (Confirmation Dessert / voted into church), and 5/31 (Confirmation Sunday).
Recap: The idea of a covenant is a central theme in the Bible. We can think about a covenant as a promise with the intent of relationship. God is the initiator of his covenant of love with humanity, and in Jesus Christ he is also the fulfiller of that covenant. We have a God who both pursues us, and refuses to let us go. In response to that great love, we are then invited to participate in God’s covenant by loving him and loving our neighbor.
The confirmation journey is a covenant between God, students and their families, and the church. God continues to grow and nurture; students and their families lean into following Jesus; the church supports and surrounds those getting confirmed.
Therefore, Confirmation requires the following commitments:
Attending class every week, or communicating when there will be an absence Attending church on Sundays Engaging in “Home Practices” Being honest If these commitments are not reasonably met, the student may not be able to get confirmed. If you are feeling challenged by this, let’s talk.
Home Practices this week:
Read Deuteronomy 7:9 with your confirmand and ask: What does it mean that God is faithful to his covenant with us? (He will always love us; he will hold us accountable and still fulfill it when we need grace or forgiveness; he will always be with us, etc.) How do we respond to that covenant faithfully? Write a covenant together regarding the student’s commitment to their faith journey and your commitment to them throughout that journey (see attached). Begin by asking/answering these three questions together:
What do you hope to learn / how do you want to grow during this journey? In what ways do you want to practice your faith during Confirmation? How do you want our family to support you in Confirmation? Parents, you are not expected to know all the answers. Investing even just a few intentional minutes to engage with your child’s faith journey is priceless.

Sent after Session 2 · Trust.
Subject: Confirmation Class — Important Date
Hello Parents,
Important Date: FALL RETREAT. Confirmands are expected to join us on the fall retreat, 11/15–11/17. The early-bird registration rate closes this Friday, 10/18. If cost is a concern, please reach out directly — there are partial scholarships available. [Sign-up link.]
Recap: These last two weeks we wrestled with the question, how do we know and trust God? If we are going to trust God, then we have to seek to know God. As we build a relationship with God and learn about how God has acted in the past through the Scriptures, we can learn to trust him even during times of uncertainty. To know God is to trust God — and to trust God is a tremendous blessing in every area of our lives.
Your turn: This week, watch this short video with your child. [Video link.]
Then share and ask these questions:
Share what it looks like in your own life to trust God. Make sure to share your struggles too. Ask: when is it easy for you to trust God? When is it difficult? Ask: what is one way you can know God better so that you might trust him more? Remember, you are not expected to know all the answers — simply to journey with your child in this faith-forming process. Even just taking ten minutes out of your day to engage with your child in this way is incredibly important in helping them develop a faith that lasts.

Sent after Session 3 · Sin.
Hello Parents,
Please join us next Sunday for a combined parent/student class.
Recap: We have just finished up unit three, where we discussed a central doctrine of the Christian faith — the doctrine of sin.
In Matthew 22:34–40, Jesus reminds us that humanity is meant to love God and love one another. Sin is both the power in the world and the practices in our lives that get in the way of those two purposes.
Sin manifests individually as well as corporately. As Christians, we recognize that our world is deeply affected by sin — but we also understand that by the power of Jesus’ resurrection, God has defeated sin and is in the process of making all things new. In the meantime, we are called to repent — turn from sin — and join God in God’s work of loving redemption.
Your turn: This week we encourage you to read Matthew 22:34–40 with your child.
Share where you have seen your family love God and love others well. Share where you have seen your family not do this well — confess and repent together. How can you do better? Invite your child to share their own thoughts. Close by praying together that God would help your family turn from sin and grow in love. Even just taking ten minutes out of your day to engage with your child in this way is incredibly important in helping them develop a faith that lasts.

Sent after Session 4 · Lord.
Hello Confirmation Parents,
Announcement: There will be no Confirmation class this Sunday, 11/17. Please pray for our retreat.
Recap: We just finished up unit four, where we discussed the question: Who is your Lord?
In Philippians 2:6–11, the Apostle Paul provides us with a beautiful hymn that describes Jesus as the Lord of all creation — a Lord who uses his infinite power for the sake of others, and who ultimately gave his life for ours. John 10:10 reminds us that Jesus is a Lord who desires to give us abundant life.
As Lord of the universe, we are called to invite Jesus to be Lord over every aspect of our lives. In all our actions and decisions, we are either declaring the lordship of Christ, or we are denying it. To deny the lordship of Jesus is to deny the abundant life he offers us. Who would want to miss that?
Your turn: This week we encourage you to read Philippians 2:1–11 and John 10:10 with your child.
Share the ways that you seek to live your life in a way that declares the lordship of Jesus. Share the areas of your life where it is easy for you to deny Jesus’ lordship. Work together with your child to brainstorm ways that your family can have the same mindset as Jesus. Close by praying together that your lives would declare the lordship of Jesus to the world. “Who is your Lord?” I encourage you to work this question into the very fabric of your family. When you are struggling with a decision, when your child is wondering how to respond to a particular circumstance, might you audibly ask together, “Who is our Lord?”

Sent after Session 5 · Salvation.
Hello Parents,
Important Dates:
Confirmands are expected to join youth group on Tuesday, 12/17 from 6:30–8:30pm. Parents are encouraged to join as well. Recap: We just finished up unit five, where we discussed the question: Who is my Savior?
In the world we are inundated with “saving” promises. Ads tell us that different products will save us from having frizzy hair to suffering the terrible fate of being irrelevant. Yet, there is only one who is truly able to save. Out of love, God sent Jesus into the world to save us from our sin and separation from God. The best news of all: we don’t earn salvation — we receive it. It is God’s gift to us. Salvation means spending eternity united with God in heaven, but it also means freedom, healing, wholeness, forgiveness, and abundant life here and now.
Your turn: Read Ephesians 2:1–9 together.
Parents, share your own salvation story:
How has God changed your life? In what ways have you experienced God’s love and grace? Then ask:
How does it make you feel knowing that God sent Jesus to save us “out of love”? Do you ever try to earn God’s love and salvation? How can we as a family remind one another of God’s unlimited grace and love for us? You don’t need to have all the answers. A ten-minute conversation can impact your child’s faith for a lifetime.

Sent after Session 6 · Disciple.
Hello Parents,
Important Dates:
Parents, please join us for next class on 1/12. In lieu of class on 1/26, the confirmands should join youth group on 1/21 at 6:30pm for mission night. (Regular Sunday school still meets on 1/26.) Recap: We just finished up unit six, where we discussed the question: What does it mean to be a disciple of Christ?
Being a disciple of Jesus leads to abundant and eternal life — but it also means sacrifice. While we find security in our relationship with God and with the people of God, we are also called to take risks. By standing against evil and standing up for God’s love in the world, we often sacrifice our own comfort and we risk being denied by the world. Being a disciple means to be a student or a follower of Jesus — the same Jesus who sacrificed it all for us.
Your turn: Read Mark 8:31–38 with your children and ask:
When does loving God and loving our neighbors call us to take risks? What is something our family could sacrifice for the good of others? What do you think Jesus means by: “For whoever wants to save their life will lose it, but whoever loses their life for me and for the gospel will save it” (v.35)? Out-of-the-box idea: Watch The Karate Kid (1984, PG) and discuss the mentor-disciple relationship between Daniel LaRusso and Mr. Miyagi. Describe the character of Mr. Miyagi and his authority. Then discuss: how is this relationship like — and different from — our relationship with Jesus?

Sent after Session 7 · Love.
Hello Confirmation Parents,
Important Dates:
Confirmands are expected to be at [location] tonight (1/21) from 6:30–8:30pm for mission night, to show Christ’s love. Therefore, no Confirmation class 1/26 (regular Sunday school still meets for all ages). Recap: We just finished up unit seven, where we discussed the question: What does it mean to show Christ’s love?
There are two different Greek words in the New Testament that are translated as love. There is phileo — a warm and affectionate love. And there is agape — a sacrificial, action-oriented love. Both loves are necessary for Christ-followers, but agape takes the highest priority, because agape is the sort of love God showed us through Jesus’ giving his life for ours.
As followers of Jesus, we respond to Christ’s love by putting our love into action and serving those around us. For our second class, we put Christ’s love in action by packing food bags for people living in motels.
Your turn: Read Colossians 3:14–17 with your children and ask:
What are ways you can put on love in your own home as a family? What is one concrete way you can put on love at school or at work? Our church shows Christ’s love in a bunch of different ways. Which way would you like to get involved in? (Mission trips, serving children, tech team, helping to lead worship, prayer, serving meals, etc.)

Sent after Session 8 · Teaching.
Hello Confirmation Parents,
Important Info:
We will be eating at [diner] together this week — send your children hungry! Our next parent class is 3/8. Recap: We just finished up unit eight, where we discussed the question: Will you devote yourself to the church’s teaching?
The church’s teaching comes first and foremost from the Scriptures. Part of following Jesus means immersing ourselves in our Holy-Spirit-inspired Bibles. The Bible teaches us both about who God is and who we are. The church has also written creeds and confessions over the course of history to concisely articulate the truths we find in Scripture. The most famous creed, shared across virtually every Christian denomination, is the Apostles’ Creed.
Over the last two weeks we also discussed the significance of weekly intergenerational worship. Aside from Sunday School, one of the primary places we learn about God is in the weekend worship service. The components of a Presbyterian worship service are selected and aligned with intentionality, and taken right from the pages of Scripture.
Your turn: Every day for the next week, read the Apostles’ Creed together.
Ask:
What comforts you about the Apostles’ Creed? What confuses you? (You don’t need to have all the answers.)
data.the-apostles-creed#The Apostles\u0026rsq:
I believe in God the Father Almighty, Maker of heaven and earth,
And in Jesus Christ his only Son our Lord; who was conceived by the Holy Ghost, born of the Virgin Mary, suffered under Pontius Pilate, was crucified, dead, and buried; he descended into hell; the third day he rose again from the dead; he ascended into heaven, and sitteth on the right hand of God the Father Almighty; from thence he shall come to judge the quick and the dead.
I believe in the Holy Ghost; the holy catholic Church; the communion of saints; the forgiveness of sins; the resurrection of the body; and the life everlasting. Amen.

data:
Sent after Session 9 · Fellowship.
Attachment: Gifts inventory sheet (Gifts.png)
Hello Parents,
Important Updates:
Confirmands and families — please sign up by 3/20 for Serve Saturday (3/28, 12:30–5:30pm). [Sign-up link.] Students, mark your calendar for 3/31 to join high-school youth group for the Holy Week reenactment. There will be no Sunday Confirmation classes until after Easter on 4/12. Recap: We just finished up unit nine, where we discussed the question: Will you devote yourself to the church’s fellowship?
The Greek word for fellowship in the New Testament is koinonia. Koinonia is not just superficially hanging out — it’s truly doing life together. This means sharing joys and burdens, caring for one another, and using our gifts to work together to advance God’s mission of reconciling the world. The koinonia of the church is like being members of the same body. Each member of the body is unique — and this diversity allows the body to function in magnificent ways. At the same time, if one member of the body suffers, every member suffers with it.
Your turn: Read 1 Corinthians 12:12–27 with your children and engage in the following discussion:
Use the attached sheet as a guide and identify gifts each person of your family has. Share how some of your own gifts have allowed you to build up the church and to bless the world. Brainstorm ways your child’s gifts can build up the church and bless the world. Pray together: “God, help us use the gifts you have given us so that we can join you at work in the world, blessing the world. In Jesus’ name, amen.”

Sent after Session 10 · Breaking Bread.
Hello Parents,
Important Dates:
Next week, 3/8, is a parent class. Recap: Last week we answered the big question: Will you devote yourself to the breaking of bread?
When we think of breaking bread together, we immediately think of Communion. Communion has past, present, and future implications. At communion we remember Jesus’ sacrifice for us on the cross — a sacrifice that reminds us of Passover, when God saved and delivered the Hebrew people from slavery through the blood of the lamb. At communion we receive God’s grace and are spiritually nourished by the Spirit of Christ present in the bread and the cup. At communion we see a foreshadowing of the great heavenly banquet table that will be hosted by Christ in eternity.
One of the most basic spiritual practices is to simply share a meal with other followers of Jesus. At the same time, we are called to welcome strangers to our own tables. As Jesus extended hospitality to us, we are called to extend hospitality to others. We make room at our tables for people different from us, because there is always room at the table of Christ.
Your turn: Read Luke 22:14–23 together and ask:
Why do we celebrate Communion / the Lord’s Supper? (To remember Jesus’ sacrifice, to experience Jesus’ Spirit now, and to anticipate the great heavenly banquet feast with Jesus in the future.) Who is invited to Jesus’ table? (Everyone.) Who would you like to invite to eat with us at our table? Take a look at your calendar. Take an inventory of how often your family is eating dinner together:
How can you increase this essential family- and faith-forming practice? How can you make it even more intentionally centered around Jesus — and his example of sharing the table as a means to faith and relationship?

Sent after Session 11 · Prayer.
Hello Parents,
Important Dates / Information:
Parents, join us for 4/19 for our last parent class. We will be going over statements of faith — don’t miss it. Please send [confirmation director] 3–4 pictures of your child by 5/1, spanning from when they were a baby up until now. These pictures will be accompanied by a voice recording of each confirmand reading a piece of their statement of faith. All of this will be compiled into a slideshow for the congregation to see on 5/31. Recap: The last two weeks we answered the big question: Will you devote yourself to prayer?
As followers of Jesus, we are to live lives of prayer. 1 Thessalonians 5:17 says “Pray without ceasing.” Prayer is both a posture we embrace and a practice we implement in our own lives. In a posture of prayer, we see all that we say, do, and think as being part of our ongoing conversation with God. In our practice of prayer, we intentionally communicate and commune with God in specific ways. The Lord’s Prayer is both the primary posture and practice Jesus demonstrated for us.
Your turn: Of course we encourage you to pray for your family — but as you are helping to form the faith of your children, we want you to commit to praying with them. Some ideas:
Share prayers at meals Pray before bed or first thing in the morning Pray after games / activities Write a family prayer together Say the Lord’s Prayer together Pray pray pray Whatever you decide to do — commit to it, model it, and invite your kids into it. The lives of your children will be tremendously blessed for it.
A simple frame: give praise to God. Ask for forgiveness for sin. Thank God. Make requests for yourself and others.

Sent in the final weeks of the year, before the statement-of-faith deadline.
Hello Confirmation Parents,
Can you believe that we are just weeks away from our students being confirmed? As our confirmands begin to write their statements of faith, we need your help.
Please make yourself available to help your children articulate their faith as they write their statements. This should be their decision and ultimately their thoughts, but as a teenager — or anyone, for that matter — this can feel like a daunting task. We are expecting a rough draft by our next class on 5/4. See the attached document for some guidance. Please send me 3–4 pictures by 5/1 of your child, spanning from when they were a baby up until now. Mark your calendars for the Confirmation Dessert, 7pm at [location] on Thursday 5/15. After students read aloud their statements of faith, the elders will vote on the confirmands becoming members of the church. Mark your calendars for Confirmation Sunday at 9:10am on Sunday 6/1 at [Church]. The students will complete their confirmation process in front of the larger congregation during the 9:30 worship service. Please invite your families to be there. Confirmation is an important time in the faith formation of our teens. While there is an end to the confirmation process, this is more of a celebration than a graduation. It is only one step in the lifelong faith journey of our students, and your participation in that faith journey is vital to them developing a lasting and impactful faith.
Please let me know if you have any questions.

Draft · still being polished Two events, both essential.
Beyond the weekly (or bi-weekly) class meetings, the Confirmation year has two set-piece events — one relational, one liturgical. Both matter. Both are logistically heavier than they look.
Confirmation DessertThe end-of-year celebration with families, mentors, and pastors draft Children & Youth SundayThe confirmands-led worship service where the statements are read aloud draft

Confirmation Sunday, led by confirmands and children.
Instead of the pastoral staff running the whole service, the confirmands and children do. Confirmands preach, read Scripture, and run the tech; the children do call-to-worship, confession, the Lord’s Prayer, and the doxology. It’s the liturgical capstone of the year.
data.confirmation-rite#Confirmation Rite:
After the sermon, the pastor invites the confirmands to stand and affirm their faith:
Baptisms (if any students are being baptized at Confirmation). The class answers the Big Four Questions together (see Session 1A). The class says the Apostles’ Creed in unison. Parents lay hands and pray over their students as the students kneel. Teachers anoint each student’s head with oil. The class is presented to the congregation as new active members.
data.roles#Roles:
Assign each role to a specific student by the end of April so they have time to prep. Roles from our recent services:
Greeters (2–3 confirmands + children) — welcome people as they arrive; hand out bulletins. These students can also be the offering collectors. Welcome / Announcements (2 confirmands) — the opening welcome; include a VBS or announcement slot for the pre-K / kindergarten group. Call to Worship — led by the children. Song leaders / band — the youth band (or a guest musician) leads songs. Confession / Pardon — led by the children. Children’s message — given by the children (plan ~8 minutes). Scripture readings (1–2 confirmands) — see the suggested list below. Sermon (1–2 confirmands) — one 10–15-minute message or two 5–7-minute messages. Offering — the greeters repeat as offering collectors. Doxology — led by the children (e.g. 2nd graders). Prayers — children sing the Lord’s Prayer with the congregation. Sending song — often Love the Lord Your God (Lincoln Brewster) with a video. Benediction (1–2 confirmands). Tech team (1–3 confirmands) — video / slides / sound, includes at least one practice Sunday.
data.sample-service-orders#Sample Service Ord: Two past examples. Names redacted; the structure is the useful part.
data.suggested-scripture-readings#Suggested S:
For the scripture-reading slot, lean toward passages on the authority and living power of Scripture (fits the “Will you devote yourself to the church’s teaching?” vow):
2 Timothy 3:16–17 — “All Scripture is God-breathed and is useful for teaching, rebuking, correcting and training in righteousness, so that the servant of God may be thoroughly equipped for every good work.” Hebrews 4:12 — “For the word of God is alive and active…” Romans 15:4 — “For everything that was written in the past was written to teach us, so that through the endurance taught in the Scriptures and the encouragement they provide we might have hope.” John 17:17 — “Sanctify them by the truth; your word is truth.” John 1:14 — “The Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us…” Deuteronomy 6:4–9 — the Shema. Psalm 119:105 — “Your word is a lamp for my feet, a light on my path.”
data.the-outline#The outline: Text — what passage will be the focal point of the message? Focus — one line about the purpose of the sermon. If you can’t say it in one line, it isn’t focused. Trouble — what is the human burden or struggle we are trying to overcome? Grace — how does God help us overcome that burden? What is the good news of what God is doing or will do? Your Move — what action do you want them to take? Coaching tips for the student preacher: write it out word-for-word first, then cut 30%. Read it out loud to a parent or mentor. Practice once standing in front of the pulpit on the Saturday before. Keep it shorter than you think.
data.the-shape#The shape: Me — something I struggle with / wrestle with. We — something we all struggle with. God — here is the solution. You — what you need to do. We — what if everybody did this? (The vision.)
data.the-student-sermon#The Student Sermon: One confirmand (or two, splitting a 15-minute message into two 7-minute pieces) preaches. Use a simple framework:
data.the-welcome#The Welcome:
Three practical notes the students should internalize:
Smile. Speak loudly and clearly. Take turns — don’t talk over each other. Two Scriptures that ground the welcome:
Romans 12:15 — “Live in harmony with one another. Do not be proud, but be willing to associate with people of low position. Do not be conceited.”
John 13:20 — “Very truly I tell you, whoever accepts anyone I send accepts me; and whoever accepts me accepts the one who sent me.”
data.the-worksheet#The worksheet:
Send the student preacher these five questions, at least a month out:
What do you want them to know? Why do you want them to know it? What do you want them to do? Why do you want them to do it? How can I help them remember it?
data.welcome-script-template#Welcome script t:
Good morning, everyone. My name is ______, and ______ and ______ are your worship hosts for the morning. Today is Children & Youth Sunday, meaning that everything is being led and has been created by the children and teens of our church.
First, we would like to invite up the kindergarteners to make a special announcement… Again, welcome to those in the sanctuary and joining us online. We are so glad you are here, especially if this is your first time with us. If you’re new, please fill out a connect card and put it in the offering plate later in the service. We would love to get to know you better. — insert any congregation-wide announcements: annual meetings, upcoming events, hospitality items, etc. — And lastly, an important word from our Pre-K class… (As they hold up a sign, the congregation says “Welcome to church! Let us stand and greet one another.”)
data.year-a-930am-service#Year A (9:30am serv: Greeters + offering collectors — 2 confirmands + children Welcome / Announcements — 2 confirmands; Pre-K / K kids’ VBS announcement Call to Worship — children Song (upbeat) — youth band Confession / Pardon — children Children’s message — children (~8 min) Scripture readings — 1–2 confirmands Sermon — 1–2 confirmands, “Journey of Faith” Offering — the greeters repeat Song (e.g. “Great Is Thy Faithfulness”) Doxology — 2nd graders Prayers — 1st graders sing the Lord’s Prayer Sending song (e.g. “Love the Lord Your God”) Benediction — 1–2 confirmands Tech team throughout — 1–3 confirmands (includes one practice Sunday the week before)
data.year-b-930am-service#Year B (9:30am serv: Greeters — 3 confirmands Welcome / Call to Worship — children Song (upbeat) — youth band Greeting Song (contemplative) — youth band Welcome again / Announcements — 2 confirmands; Pre-K / K VBS announcement Confession / Pardon — children Song (refrain only) — youth band Children’s message — children (~8 min) Scripture readings — 2 confirmands Sermon — 2 confirmands, “Journey of Faith” (one 15-min or two 7-min) Offering — 4 confirmands (can repeat the greeters) Song Doxology — 2nd graders Prayers — 1st graders sing the Lord’s Prayer Sending song Benediction — 2 confirmands Tech team — 3 confirmands

data:
The end-of-year celebration.
The week before Confirmation Sunday, families, mentors, and pastors gather for dessert. Each confirmand is introduced by a teacher, lights a candle, and reads their statement of faith aloud to the room. At the end, the elders vote to receive the class into membership.
data.confirmation-sunday-next-day#Confirmatio: Students and families arrive by 9am. The students are leading worship, and families will want to get a seat before the service starts. Students sit with families, dispersed equally on either aisle. After the sermon, the pastor invites the confirmands to stand and affirm their faith. If any students are being baptized, that happens here. Parents lay hands and pray over their students as the students kneel. Teachers anoint each student’s head with oil. See the Children & Youth Sunday page for the full service structure.
data.logistics#Logistics: Dress: semi-formal / business casual Arrive: 6:15pm to help set up Event: 7–8:30pm (may run to 9pm) Location: your youth-ministry space or the church sanctuary
data.prep#Prep: All statements of faith are ready to be read. Timeline: End of April — begin distributing statements of faith for draft 2nd-to-last week in May — statements of faith due Last week in May — final statements (must be edited and locked by this date) Voice recordings of statements submitted for the Confirmation Sunday slideshow Each teacher owns a few confirmands to introduce at the Dessert Retrieve full names of confirmands and parents for the session vote and the worship service
data.run-of-show#Run-of-Show:
Host opens — welcomes everyone, explains what’s going to happen. Introduce the teachers. A teacher begins introducing the confirmands (teachers alternate). Each teacher says a few words about each confirmand. The confirmand is invited up, lights a tea candle from the Christ light (put the match at the top of the flame, then pick up the tea light to light it — symbolizing the light of Christ in all of us), and reads their statement of faith at the microphone. After all statements have been read, the host opens the floor to questions from session. Session votes to receive the confirmands as members of the church. Host talks briefly about Confirmation Sunday — arrival time, dress, seating, what the confirmands will do. Close in prayer. Mentor introductions. Each teacher writes a brief intro for each student they own — usually 3–4 sentences. Name the strengths you’ve seen in them over the year; a specific memory or two; one thing you’re praying for them going forward. Keep it honest and short. Example structure:
"[Confirmand], I first met you at [event / moment]. This year I’ve watched you [specific observation]. What has struck me most is [quality or trait]. I’ve been blessed to [something specific you’ve shared — a game, a conversation, a prayer]. I can’t wait to see how God uses you."
data.supplies#Supplies: Dessert (session brings) Christ light (Paschal candle) on the central table Tea lights — one per confirmand A central table with matches, tea lights, and the Christ light, where confirmands read 8 tables with white tablecloths Microphone for statements
data.who-is-invited#Who Is Invited: Students and their parents / siblings Confirmation teachers Session (the congregation’s elders) Pastors Confirmation director HS youth leader Any immediate family the students want to invite Confirmation teachers invite students and families. The confirmation director notifies the clerk of session by the end of April so session can be invited. Session brings the dessert.

data:
Draft · still being polished The operational playbook for leading a year of Confirmation.
The lessons are the heart of Confirmation, but they don’t fly without a calendar, a roster, a group chat, and two adult guides (one for the lead teacher, one for student mentors). This is the scaffolding every Confirmation director wishes had been handed to them on day one.
data.in-this-section#In this section: Sample Calendar — a full-year schedule, student-facing and parent-facing Roster & Parent Contacts — spreadsheet templates for the year Attendance — a tidy attendance tracker Group Chat Setup — how we do the student group chat and why Teacher’s Guide — the lead teacher’s overview of the whole curriculum Mentor’s Guide — Big God, Big Questions — what to hand each adult mentor Every document in this section is a template. Copy it, change the dates, swap the names, and use it. The only thing church-specific in these files is the church name, and those are called out.

data:
A simple attendance tracker.
One row per student, one column per session. A few extra columns for retreat attendance, Serve Saturday, and the Confirmation Dessert.
data.columns#Columns: Student name (preferred name for day-to-day, plus a separate full-name column for official records) Each session date (1A, 1B, 2A, 2B, … 12) Fall retreat Serve Saturday Confirmation Dessert
data.how-to-use-it#How to use it: Mark P (present), A (absent), or E (excused) each Sunday. At the end of January and again in April, take five minutes with the roster — any student with more than 3 unexcused absences gets a phone call. Pastoral, not punitive. The tracker is also useful when a parent asks in April: “How many classes has my kid missed?” and you can answer without guessing. 📄 Downloadable template lives with the other operational spreadsheets. Any simple tab-per-year spreadsheet works.

data:
How we run the Confirmation group chat.
A group chat is the single most useful tool for keeping a Confirmation class connected between meetings. We use a purpose-built chat inside our church’s app so that every thread has an adult in it, parents can be looped in, and messages stay accessible to both students and leaders.
data.ground-rules-for-the-chat#Ground Rules f:
Write these up in the first message when the group is created:
A leader is always in this chat. Talk like you would in front of an adult — because you are. No bullying, no one-on-one DMs between adults and students. Use the chat to share prayer requests, schedule reminders, and questions from class.
data.if-your-church-doesnt-have-an-app#If You:
Common alternatives:
GroupMe — platform-agnostic, doesn’t require a phone number to invite. A private Discord server — easy for leaders to moderate channels; overkill for smaller classes. Signal group — simple, secure, but requires a phone number. Whichever you use: a leader is in the group, parents know it exists, and safe-practices guidelines are followed.
data.instructions-for-students#Instructions f:
Hand this out at Kickoff (or screenshot and text to the class chat if it already exists):
Download the church app. Search for your church name in the Apple App Store or Google Play Store. Press Get or Download. Open the app and click the person icon in the top-right corner. Press “Log in or sign up” and create an account. Once signed in, return to the home screen and press the messaging icon in the top-right corner. Press Discover and search Confirmation. Click the Confirmation group, click Join Group, and then View in Messaging. The group chat will now be available under Conversations.

data:
For each mentor.
Every confirmand gets an adult mentor for the year — someone from the congregation who commits to meeting with, praying for, and eventually speaking a blessing over them at the Confirmation Dessert. Mentors are the single most underrated part of a Confirmation year.
We use the published Big God, Big Questions Home / Mentor Guide by Geneva Press. That resource is copyrighted and not reproduced on this site — give each mentor a copy. What follows is our own church-specific guidance on picking and supporting mentors.
data.monthly-rhythm#Monthly rhythm:
A simple pattern to share with mentors:
Early fall — introduce yourself. Find out what they like to do. Let them know what you’re praying for them about. Mid-year — walk through the Big God, Big Questions mentor-guide prompt for the current session. Listen more than you talk. Lent / spring — check in on the statement of faith. Be a safe person to read the first, terrified draft. May — write your blessing for the Dessert. Show up. Mean it.
data.selecting-mentors#Selecting mentors:
From the Big God, Big Questions Teacher’s Guide, adapted:
A mentor needs to be accessible to their confirmand on a regular basis. Except for unique circumstances, connect confirmands to mentors who are not members of their own family. Take a young person’s pastoral needs into account when pairing. The most important qualifications for a mentor are not piety or orthodoxy, but love and grace. Every adult who works with children and youth must be screened through a criminal background check. Contact your congregation’s insurance representative. Check with your pastor or Christian educator about your church’s safe-practices policy for youth. Meeting in a coffee shop or at church when others are present is usually the right default.
data.supporting-mentors#Supporting mentors:
Mentor burnout is real. The lead teacher should:
Text mentors the week of each Confirmation class they’re expected at (don’t assume the calendar is enough). Email mentors a 2-sentence summary after each session so they can bring it up naturally in their next check-in. Check in with mentors by phone twice a year. Some need encouragement; some need to gracefully tag out.
data.what-mentors-commit-to#What mentors comm: Attending the sessions where mentors are expected: Session 1 (Kickoff / Covenant), Session 4 (Lord), Session 8 (Teaching), and Session 12 (Crossing Over). The session pages flag these. A monthly check-in with their confirmand — a coffee, a walk, or a shared activity. No agenda required beyond “how’s your faith going?” Praying for their confirmand by name, every day, all year. Speaking a blessing over their confirmand at the Confirmation Dessert.

data:
Student roster and parent contact templates.
Two simple spreadsheets the lead teacher keeps current throughout the year.
data.parent-contacts#Parent contacts:
One row per confirmand household:
Student name Parent(s) / guardian(s) name Phone(s) Email(s) Preferred contact channel — text, email, app Household notes — divorced, shared custody, grandparent-in-the-loop, etc. (Keep this respectful and minimal.)
data.privacy#Privacy:
This data is pastoral and should be treated as such. Don’t share it outside the Confirmation team. At the end of the year, archive in a secured location and clear the working copy.
📄 Downloadable templates for both sheets live alongside the calendar in the source repo. Your church may already have a preferred format — if so, use that.
data.student-roster#Student roster:
One row per confirmand:
Full name (including middle name — needed for Confirmation Sunday) Preferred name (what they actually go by) Grade / school Baptism status — baptized already, or to be baptized at Confirmation Baptism date (if known) Mentor (assigned by late October) Attendance — link to the attendance sheet Statement of faith — draft / revised / final / read aloud Photo collection — Y/N (for the Confirmation Sunday slideshow)

data:
A full-year Confirmation schedule.
This is the master calendar we use. It covers every class session, the fall retreat, Confirmation Dessert, Confirmation Sunday, and the parent touchpoints in between. Copy it, shift the dates, and adapt to your own program.
data.downloads#Downloads: 📄 Downloadable templates. The source calendar spreadsheet and parent-facing PDF live in the source repo under the year they were used. Ask the maintainer to publish a recent year’s copy if you need one.
data.planning-tips#Planning tips: Sync with your church’s master calendar before you commit to dates. Mission trips, Holy Week events, and vacation-week breaks will eat your Sunday mornings. Build in flex weeks. Snow days, graduations, and unexpected illnesses will cost you a session or two. Having 2–3 “no class” weeks scattered through the year gives you room. Pair each class with its parent email. Send the email the week after, while the topic is fresh. Put the fall retreat before Session 4. The retreat bonds the class; after it, the deep-cut sessions (Lord, Salvation, Disciple) land differently.
data.typical-shape-of-a-year#Typical shape of: September — invites go out to rising 9th graders; Kickoff (Session 1A) Early October — first parent/student class (Session 1B Covenant) October–November — Sessions 2 (Trust) and 3 (Sin) Mid-November — Fall Retreat (Friday–Sunday) December — Sessions 4 (Lord) and 5 (Salvation), with a break for Christmas January–February — Sessions 6 (Disciple), 7 (Love) March — Session 8 (Teaching), Serve Saturday, Session 9 (Fellowship) Early April — Holy Week reenactment; break for Easter April–May — Sessions 10 (Breaking Bread), 11 (Prayer); statement-of-faith drafting Late May — Session 12 (Crossing Over); Confirmation Dessert End of May / early June — Confirmation Sunday (Children & Youth Sunday)

data:
For the lead teacher.
The Sessions on this site are a Presbyterian-flavored adaptation of a published curriculum, Big God, Big Questions: Confirmation for a Growing Faith by Geneva Press (Presbyterian Church USA). That teacher’s guide, the BIG Journal (student workbook), the mentor guide, and the infographic poster set are the commercial resources we build on.
data.free-previews#Free previews: Scope & Sequence PDF — the 12-session arc at a glance Sample book — free to order The published Big God, Big Questions curriculum is copyrighted by Geneva Press (© 2018, Louisville, KY). This site does not reproduce it. What’s published here — in the Sessions section — is our own teaching notes and adaptations for each topic, in our own words. If you plan to run a full Confirmation year, we strongly recommend buying the official resource: it’s thorough, theologically careful, and gives you the BIG Journal and infographics as student-facing pieces.
data.how-to-prep-each-session#How to prep eac:
Each session page on this site follows the actual flow a teacher works through:
Before class — supplies, setup, guests to invite Opening / icebreaker — warm up the room Read — the scripture + context you teach around Discuss — the questions with answer hints for the teacher Activity — the hands-on or group piece Close — how to send them out Read the page a few days ahead so you know what props to gather (clay, bread, bowls of water, a 2×4, the right video cued up). Many sessions have optional extensions you can pull in if discussion runs short.
data.pacing#Pacing:
Most sessions are split into a Part A and Part B, taught across two class meetings two weeks apart. A handful (9, 10, 12) are single meetings. All told, expect ~22 class meetings plus a fall retreat, Confirmation Dessert, and Confirmation Sunday.
See the Sample Calendar for a concrete year’s worth of dates.
data.the-arc-at-a-glance#The arc at a glance:
Twelve sessions, anchored in the four Big Questions asked at a PCUSA Confirmation:
Trusting in the gracious mercy of God, do you turn from the ways of sin and renounce evil and its power in the world? Who is your Lord and Savior? Will you be Christ’s faithful disciple, obeying his Word and showing his love? Will you devote yourself to the church’s teaching and fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers? The twelve sessions map to those vows:
Covenant — Joshua 24 Trust — Psalm 105; Hebrews 11 Sin — Matthew 22:34–40; Genesis 3 Lord — Philippians 2; Exodus 3; 1 Kings 18 Salvation — Ephesians 2; Exodus 3; Luke 19; 2 Kings 5; Mark 2; Matthew 18; Luke 15 Disciple — Mark 8; Mark 12; Matthew 20; Acts 7 Love — John 21 Teaching — 2 Timothy 1; Hebrews 12 Fellowship — 1 Corinthians 12; John 13 Breaking Bread — Luke 22; Exodus 12; Acts 10 Prayer — Luke 18; Colossians 4; 1 Thessalonians 5; Psalm 51; Romans 5 Crossing Over — Joshua 3; Romans 6
data.the-statement-of-faith-project#The state: Introduced in Session 12 and read aloud at the Confirmation Dessert. See the Statement of Faith section for the framework, guides, and examples.
data.where-to-buy#Where to buy: Curriculum overview Teacher’s Guide BIG Journal (student workbook) Home / Mentor Guide Infographics (set of 16 posters) DVD / video set Class Kit (everything bundled)
data.working-with-mentors#Working with mentor: Every confirmand gets an adult mentor. Hand each mentor the Mentor’s Guide at the start of the year. The session pages flag where mentors are expected (Session 1, Session 4, Session 8, Session 12) — make sure those dates are on mentors’ calendars well in advance.
data.working-with-parents#Working with parent: The rhythm of parent emails runs parallel to the sessions. Send the matching email the week after each topic. It gives parents a Home Practice to try with their confirmand and keeps the dinner-table theology going between classes.

data:
Draft · sessions are still being polished The twelve-topic Confirmation curriculum.
Twelve topics, most of them split across two class meetings (Part A + Part B) so students have a week between them to sit with the question. The last four topics (Teaching, Fellowship, Breaking Bread, Prayer) are the four Acts 2:42 vows a confirmand takes as part of becoming a member. The final session — Crossing Over — is the statement-of-faith writing workshop.
data.the-arc#The arc: Covenant — what God has always been up to Trust — knowing and trusting God Sin — naming what’s broken Lord — Jesus as Lord, not just teacher Salvation — who is your Savior? Disciple — following, not just admiring Love — the shape of Christ’s love Teaching — will you devote yourself to the apostles’ teaching? Fellowship — …and to the fellowship? Breaking Bread — …and to the breaking of bread? Prayer — …and to the prayers? Crossing Over — writing a statement of faith Pacing. With A/B pairs, sessions 1–9 and 11 take two meetings each; sessions 10 and 12 take one each. That’s 22 meetings across the academic year — a bi-weekly cadence from September through May with room for a retreat, the Confirmation Dessert, and Confirmation Sunday. 🛠️ Content import is in progress. Each session page below is a structural placeholder today. As sessions are converted from the source Word documents, this callout will go away.

data:
Part A of Session 1 · Covenant.
The first night. Nobody yet knows how this goes. Your job tonight is to set a tone — warm, honest, no-phones, no-dumb-questions — and to plant the word covenant in every kid’s head before they walk out.
data.before-class#Before class: Write down attendance and share it with the confirmation director Pens and slips of paper ready for the closing reflection Bibles for the Joshua 24 reading Whiteboard or phone notes ready for the ground-rules list
data.close#Close:
Say:
The next class is next week and your parents are expected to attend. (The confirmation director will email parents as well.)
Pray out together as a class.
data.confirmation-is-a-journey#Confirmation I:
Say:
Confirmation is not meant to be like school, or a class — it’s meant to be a journey of faith together. It’s a journey where we learn and practice together what it means to be a Christian and to follow Jesus.
At the end of confirmation, if you so choose, you’ll have the opportunity to make a public profession of faith and to join the church.
It’s also a journey we take together.
Have everyone join the confirmation group chat. (See the Group Chat Setup page for the how-and-why.)
data.ground-rules#Ground Rules:
We also want to create a few ground rules. Teacher, write these down in a note in your phone. (Examples: listen louder than you speak, no putdowns…)
Ask:
What would be some good ground rules?
Teachers, make sure to add:
Everyone opens or closes in prayer at least once What happens at Confirmation stays at Confirmation — unless it’s a danger to you or someone else No question is a dumb question No phones
data.opening--go-around#Opening — Go-Around:
Have everyone share (including teachers):
Name School they attend Highs and lows from the summer Do you have any siblings? What order do you fall in? What’s cool about that? What’s not so cool about that?
data.read--joshua-24#Read — Joshua 24:
Say:
Today we’re going to briefly look at a passage in the Bible in the book of Joshua that deals with commitments and promises from God to God’s people, the nation of Israel. The Bible is the story of God’s people that leads us to Jesus.
Joshua was the leader of Israel after Moses. The Israelites were enslaved by Pharaoh in Egypt. Then God used Moses to lead them out of slavery, but they spent forty years wandering in the desert.
Finally they come to a new place called Canaan, but Moses dies and Joshua becomes their leader. The people in the land of Canaan follow other gods and idols — they have statues they believe are connected to other gods, and they worship those statues. Israel would fall into this same practice in the future.
But before this can happen, God uses Joshua to speak to the people about their covenant with God and how they should live.
Pass around Bibles. Choose a few readers and read:
Joshua 24:1–3, 13–15, 22–25 Ask:
What do you think a covenant is? (v.25) A covenant is a promise for the sake of relationship. God makes a covenant with us to love us and always be with us. We fulfill that covenant — that promise — by loving God and loving others. What do you think it means to “put away the foreign gods that are among you, and incline your hearts to the Lord, the God of Israel” (v.23)? What are some examples of foreign gods today? What are some things we can do to be Joshua for each other and stand firm in our commitment to love God and love others throughout this confirmation journey? What promises do you think you’ll be asked to make at Confirmation?
data.the-big-four#The Big Four:
Say:
Over the next several months we’ll be exploring the big four questions that you’ll be asked on the day of your confirmation in June:
Trusting in the gracious mercy of God, do you turn from the ways of sin and renounce evil and its power in the world? Who is your Lord and Savior? Will you be Christ’s faithful disciple, obeying his Word and showing his love? Will you devote yourself to the church’s teaching and fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers?
data.would-you-rather#Would You Rather?:
Ask:
Would you rather have to break a promise, or have a promise broken to you? Explain.
data.written-reflection#Written Reflection:
Pass out pens and slips of paper. Write the following two questions on the board and have students answer them. They can — but don’t have to — write their names on the slips. Collect the slips afterwards.
What is one thing you want to get out of your confirmation journey? What questions do you have about God? Ask:
Is anyone open to sharing what they wrote?

data:
Part B of Session 1 · Covenant.
First night with parents in the room. Names still being learned. Tonight you get the whole Confirmation year onto one page: what a covenant is, why it matters, what the three-way confirmation means, and what you’re asking of students, parents, and leaders.
data.appendix--full-name-sign-up-sheet#Append:
Print a single-sheet handout with the following header and a numbered list of 20 blank lines for families to fill in at the end of class:
Parents’ and students’ full names (including middle names)
Write a “B!” if the confirmand HAS been baptized.
data.at-tables#At tables:
People introduce themselves by counting off 1–4 and answering the question on the screen:
Biggest animal you’ve seen in person Biggest group of people you’ve spoken in front of Longest road trip or flight Biggest decision you’ve ever made
data.before-class#Before class: Name tags for everyone Printed list for people to write full names (students + parents; mark “B!” if the confirmand has been baptized). A template appendix is at the bottom of this page. Printed Confirmation calendars for parents Note cards and pens on tables Tell students to sit with their family
data.big-group#Big group: Find out who in the room has seen the biggest animal, spoken in front of the biggest group, taken the longest trip, and made the biggest decision.
data.close#Close:
Say:
I’ve done a ton of talking — but for the remainder of the year, this will be much more of a discussion. We want to be a group of fellow travelers learning more about God and one another on this faith journey.
Hand out:
Parent calendars Bagel sign-up Before you go: have each family write full names of parents and students, and mark with “B!” if the confirmand has been baptized.
Ask: Are there any questions?
Pray out.
data.discussion--promises-and-commitments#Dis:
At tables:
What are some promises and commitments you’ve made or expect to make one day? (i.e. pledge of allegiance, honor code at school, marriage, etc.) Why do we make promises? What difference does a promise make in the way we act? What happens when we break promises? Who is hurt? Each group shares a few answers with the entire class.
data.introductions#Introductions: Introduce the teachers. “We’ll take turns leading, and today I’m up.” Today we want to: Get to know one another Explore the meaning of Confirmation Discuss the commitments necessary for this journey
data.our-commitment-to-you#Our Commitment to: We’re committed to walking with you and your family in faith. The teachers will divide up the class and check in with you regularly. We’re committed to wrestling alongside students as they explore their faith and ask questions. We’ll send an email every other week recapping what we learned and giving you a practice for at home.
data.parents#Parents: Thank you for driving your kids. It is exhausting to drive to practice, drive to youth group, drive to Sunday School, drive them to friends’ houses. Thank you, thank you, thank you. Lead your son or daughter by example. Attend worship. Pray at meals. Model what it means to follow Jesus. Be honest about your faith, including the struggles and the doubts. And attend the few parent classes on the calendar. Communicate with us regularly and spend some time each week engaging in your student’s Confirmation journey. We know you have a lot going on, so we want to make that as easy as possible.
data.read--covenant#Read — Covenant:
Say:
The reason we’re talking about promises is because the Confirmation class journey is all about covenant.
We first see the term covenant in the Old Testament. Covenant is a legal term that binds two parties together. In the Bible, we see that God initiates the covenant with us. It’s a promise with the intent of relationship.
We see this covenant-making between God and humanity all over Scripture, but look with me to Deuteronomy 7:9.
Deuteronomy 7:9 — “Know therefore that the Lord your God is God; he is the faithful God, keeping his covenant of love to a thousand generations of those who love him and keep his commandments.”
Say:
There are two parties in this divine covenant: God and us.
Scripture describes God’s covenant with us — and we know God meant what he said because: Jesus.
Jesus coming and walking among us, teaching us how to live and to love, and then giving his life to pay the cost of our sin, freeing us from being separated from God — Jesus is the fulfillment of God’s covenant to us. God makes his covenant and fulfills his end of it, because he loves us.
We’re going to spend this Confirmation journey getting to know God through Jesus.
Who is God? Who does God say we are? And how has God called each of you to partner with him in the world?
Confirmation is a time for asking questions — so ask all of the questions you want.
data.students#Students: Attendance each week. (You can miss if you’re sick, if there’s a special event, or a family gathering — but make this a priority.) Be in church. Engage with your parents at home. Be honest. No phones.
data.the-big-four-preview#The Big Four (Previ:
Say:
You will be asked what we call the big four questions — questions we’ll spend the entire year talking about and looking to see what the Bible has to say about them.
So:
Confirmation isn’t a graduation. It might be a new stage in your faith. It might be the start of making your faith your own. But it isn’t meant to end here. We want to learn, but we also want to have fun. Life with God is an adventure. Because faith is a journey we all take together — and like any journey, it requires commitment.
data.the-three-way-confirmation#The Three-Way:
Say:
So that’s God’s part — but what about us? Relationships take two. Like the text we read, it goes both ways. While God is the initiator and the ultimate fulfiller of the covenant, he invites us to respond by following him and loving his people.
Ask:
Who here has been baptized? Who hasn’t? (If you haven’t, you’ll have the opportunity.) Does anyone know why we get baptized? Say:
Individuals are received into the church to share in its life and ministry. It’s a statement of your identity as a beloved child of God — of God choosing you. At baptism, the parents, the sponsors, and the congregation publicly promise to help raise these (typically) infant children into adults. Those adults include Sunday School teachers, mentors at the youth ministry, and every adult in the congregation. Say:
So other than being an example of a covenantal promise, why am I talking about baptism?
Because going through Confirmation is really a three-way confirmation:
The Holy Spirit confirms our baptismal identity as a child of God. The church confirms your gifts and membership in the body of Christ. You confirm your faith in Jesus and the teachings of the church. At the end of this process, this is your decision.
data.welcome-and-prayer#Welcome and Prayer:
Welcome to Confirmation. Let’s pray.
Ground rules (quick recap):
Be kind, be present (phones away) Safe space Everyone prays once No question is a dumb question

data:
Part A of Session 2 · Trust.
Overview. When God calls us into covenant relationship, God calls us to trust. When we understand how God has acted in the past, is acting in the present, and promises to act tomorrow, we can trust in God’s promises even in the face of uncertainty. When we come to know God, we come to trust God.
The big question for the next two weeks: How do we know and trust God?
Two ways to run this session. Source material includes two complete variants — pick the one that fits your space and weather.
Outdoor (recommended): Start with a blindfolded trust walk in a park, then teach the psalm. Song (Oceans) moves to Session 2B. Indoor: Skip the trust walk, go straight into discussion, and include Oceans in this session. Both variants are laid out below. Sections marked “Outdoor only” or “Indoor only” are variant-specific; everything else is shared.
data.before-class#Before class: Prep teachers with the testimony questions (see below) Note cards for questions at the end Print song lyrics (Oceans by Hillsong United) — indoor variant Bluetooth speaker — indoor variant Blindfolds, blankets, two Bibles — outdoor variant Text confirmands in advance: dress warmly, wear sneakers — outdoor variant
data.close#Close:
Say:
The journey of faith is about more and more deeply trusting God — and so it’s about more and more deeply knowing God through building relationship with him.
Close with worship. Ask a volunteer (or rotate through the circle) to read Psalm 105:1–5 one line at a time.
data.discussion--knowing-and-trusting#Discuss:
Say:
Trust always comes through relationships. Just like with anyone, before we can trust God, we have to know God.
Ask:
Who do you know best in the world? How do you know them best? How can you know someone? (You spend time with them now. You look at the patterns they’ve shown in the past.)
data.ground-rules#Ground Rules:
Say:
First, we want to create a circle of trust here, so we want to come up with some ground rules — a covenant we want to make with one another.
Examples:
No question is a dumb question Everyone has to pray at least once (last one in the room has to pray) Listen louder than you speak What happens here stays here — unless it’s a danger to you or someone else Three put-ups for a put-down No cursing Get the group chat going (if it’s not already running from Session 1 — see Group Chat Setup).
data.icebreaker#Icebreaker:
Outdoor only — the trust walk:
Blindfolded walk through the park where first the partner guide can touch them, then only their voice, and they are spun around at various times.
Ask:
What was hard about that? Where did trust come in? How was that walk like following God? Both variants — go-around:
Name Where you fall in the birth order of your siblings What’s cool about that? What’s not so cool about that? Indoor only — add:
Who do you trust most in the world? Why? What does it take to build trust? To lose it?
data.read--psalm-105#Read — Psalm 105:
Say:
The Bible is one of the ways we can know who God is — especially by looking at who God has been in the past.
The book of Psalms is a book of songs and poems used in the worship life of God’s people, from the Old Testament through today. It’s been called “a Bible within the Bible” because there’s so much in it — joy, sadness, anger, the past, the present, the future. Today we’ll look at one of the psalms that sings of the past.
Ask volunteers to read aloud from Psalm 105:
Abraham and Sarah — Psalm 105:7–11 Joseph — Psalm 105:16–22 (outdoor variant) or Psalm 105:16–25 (indoor variant) Moses and the exodus — Psalm 105:37–41 (outdoor) or Psalm 105:26–36 (indoor) Ask:
What does the psalm tell you about God being trustworthy? Gracious? Merciful? Say:
Knowing how someone has typically acted in the past is helpful — but does that mean we fully know them?
We have to have relationship with them now.
(Outdoor variant:) How can we do that?
data.teacher-testimony#Teacher Testimony:
Say:
We hope you can trust us teachers — but now we want to talk about what it looks like to trust God. First, we’ll share a little bit of our own faith journey.
Have teachers share openly and briefly on these questions:
Describe what it looks and feels like to know and trust God. When did you have a tough time trusting God? How did you come to know and trust God? (What things have helped you be close to God / know God?)
data.the-promises-of-god#The Promises of God:
Say:
As we come to trust God, we come to be able to trust in the promises of God.
One current promise of God:
Matthew 28:20b — “And remember, I am with you always, to the end of the age.”
One future promise of God:
Revelation 21:4 — “He will wipe every tear from their eyes. Death will be no more; mourning and crying and pain will be no more, for the first things have passed away.”
data.worship--oceans-indoor-only#Worship — Oc:
Listen to the song together — “Oceans (Where Feet May Fail)” by Hillsong United. Listen for ways the author talks about trusting or growing their trust in God.
Listen: YouTube · Spotify · Apple Music Read along: Genius lyrics Lyric display in class. If you want to project or print the lyrics, make sure your church’s CCLI license covers it. “Oceans (Where Feet May Fail)” is copyrighted by Hillsong Music Publishing; we don’t reproduce the lyrics on this open-source site. Ask:
How do you call upon the name of God? How does your soul rest in God’s embrace? How do we invite the Spirit to lead us? To be in the presence of our Savior?

data:
Part B of Session 2 · Trust.
The big question continues: How do we know and trust God?
Pair this session with the variant you ran last week.
If you did the outdoor trust walk in 2A, include Oceans here and skip the indoor trust-walk activity. If you did the indoor variant of 2A, include the indoor trust-walk activity here. Both paths are marked below.
data.discussion--do-you-trust-god#Discussion:
Ask:
Do you trust God? Why? Why not? Can you always depend on God? What about when sad things happen? Is God still being dependable then? Why is it important for confirmands to trust that God is gracious and merciful? Continuum exercise (after-outdoor variant):
Have students stand in the middle of the room. Read each statement — students move to one side if they agree, the other if they disagree, or stay in the middle if they’re unsure.
God is always with you. God has a good plan for you. God always hears your prayers. When bad things happen, you trust it is always going to work out.
data.get-up-and-move--would-you-rather#Get Up:
Stand in the middle of the room. Ask each question; students move to a side of the room based on their answer.
Would you rather eat sushi ten days past its expiration date, or chew pre-chewed gum found on a New York City subway? Would you rather drink a tablespoon of pond water, or a ten-year-old bottle of expired soda? Would you rather eat sardines every day for a week, or always use the same fork, spoon, and knife used by every person in a local restaurant — without being washed? Would you rather cross an ocean in an inflatable raft, or fly across the continent in an airplane that had been in a crash? Would you rather have ten arms or ten legs? Now come back and sit down.
Ask:
Why would it be hard to trust eating, drinking, or traveling in these things? Why do these choices gross us out or make us nervous when we think about them? What are some reasons we wouldn’t trust eating, drinking, or using these items? Share these concepts:
When we believe something will make us sick, or that something is unreliable to use, we cannot trust it. We want to avoid those items at all costs. Things in this world will fall apart or go bad, and we have a hard time trusting something that we think could cause us harm or put us in a difficult situation. (And the last question — I was really just curious what you’d answer.) Only God is completely trustworthy. He deserves our trust and confidence. And as we come to know him, we will come to trust him.
data.opening#Opening:
Ask: Someone refresh me on what I missed last week.
Ask: What is trust?
Firm belief in the reliability, truth, ability, or strength of someone or something.
Ask: What are some actual practices or ways you can build your relationship with God? Ways to know God better?
Possible answers: read the Bible, pray, show love to others, worship, listen to Christian music, obey, serve, live with gratitude, seek like-minded people, live intentionally, live with optimism.
data.read--hebrews-11#Read — Hebrews 11:
Say:
Let’s look at one small part of the Bible that lays down why to trust and who did.
Hebrews 11:1–38 is a list of the faithful who have trusted God “by faith.” (Faith and trust in God can be used interchangeably.)
Quick background: the book of Hebrews is a letter in the New Testament written to remind the people to remain faithful to Jesus. Two main goals: first, to show Jesus as superior — worthy of total trust and devotion; second, to remind the people to remain faithful to Jesus even through hard times.
Ask volunteers to read:
Noah — Hebrews 11:7 (cross-reference Genesis 6:13–22) Abraham — Hebrews 11:8–9 (Genesis 12:1–8) Sarah — Hebrews 11:11 (Genesis 21:1–7) Abraham again — Hebrews 11:17–18 (Genesis 22:1–18) Moses — Hebrews 11:23–29 (Exodus 14:21–30) Rahab — Hebrews 11:31 (Joshua 2:1–14) Ask:
What does Hebrews 11 tell you about God’s people? About God in your life? Based on what you’ve found in these stories, what do you think faith — or by faith — means? Read aloud Hebrews 11:1–3. How does this help us fill out the definition of faith?

data.activity--missing-the-mark#Activity — Mi:

Ask:

data.little-s-sin-vs-big-s-sin#Little-s sin v:
Say:
There are the little-s sins we commit and the big-S Sin that is a power — a power that causes sins and separation from God.
The Bible talks about big-S Sin as caused by three things:
Satan — symbolized by the snake in many traditions. Supernatural powers of evil that try to lie and distort things. Flesh — our own misled desires or even thoughts. The World — the influence of others around us. We’ll continue with our response to sin next week.
data.opening#Opening:
Ask:
What are the sort of things that break relationships? When you hear the word sin, what comes to mind?
data.read--genesis-316#Read — Genesis 3:1–6:
Genesis 3:1–6 — Now the serpent was more crafty than any of the wild animals the Lord God had made. He said to the woman, “Did God really say, ‘You must not eat from any tree in the garden’?”
The woman said to the serpent, “We may eat fruit from the trees in the garden, but God did say, ‘You must not eat fruit from the tree that is in the middle of the garden, and you must not touch it, or you will die.’”
“You will not certainly die,” the serpent said to the woman. “For God knows that when you eat from it your eyes will be opened, and you will be like God, knowing good and evil.”
When the woman saw that the fruit of the tree was good for food and pleasing to the eye, and also desirable for gaining wisdom, she took some and ate it. She also gave some to her husband, who was with her, and he ate it.
data.read--matthew-223440#Read — Matthew 22:3:
Matthew 22:34–40 — Hearing that Jesus had silenced the Sadducees, the Pharisees got together. One of them, an expert in the law, tested him with this question: “Teacher, which is the greatest commandment in the Law?”
Jesus replied: “‘Love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind.’ This is the first and greatest commandment. And the second is like it: ‘Love your neighbor as yourself.’ All the Law and the Prophets hang on these two commandments.”
Ask:
Who is in the passage? Who can give a summary of what happened? What relationships do the greatest commandments talk about? Draw or reference a cross: there’s our vertical relationship with God and our horizontal relationship with people.
Say:
Sin always means broken relationships. We miss the mark when we don’t love God and neighbor.
Ask: Does anyone do these things perfectly? No — we all miss the mark.
data.video--bibleproject-on-sin-khata#Video —:
Play the BibleProject short video on the Hebrew word for sin, khata (to miss the mark): https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/khata-sin/
Ask:
Did anything jump out to you about the video? What do you think it means that every hero of the Bible sinned — except Jesus? It means God expects us to miss the mark, and loves us anyway. God uses imperfect people to do extraordinary things. Forgiveness is always available.

data: Part B of Session 3 · Sin.
data.before-class#Before class: Confirmation ground rules; collect phones Bibles for all Notebooks (if using the Pg 23 / Pg 27 resources) or printed equivalents
data.close#Close:
Say:
We all miss the mark, and there’s a consequence to our sin. Jesus takes those consequences onto himself and offers us forgiveness — and sends us out to bring life and repair.
As we spend time with Jesus and his people, it becomes easier and easier to follow him.
The first of the Big Four vows — this is what we’ve been building toward:
Trusting in the gracious mercy of God, do you turn from the way of sin and renounce evil and its power in the world?
Close in prayer.
data.discussion#Discussion:
Ask:
Is it a sin to lie? Why? Is it a sin to not help someone who falls and hurts themselves? Why? Is it a sin if everyone pollutes the lake and the drinking water gets contaminated? Why? Is it a sin if there is a law that prevents people with the name Bob from getting into college? Why?
data.does-god-punish-us-for-our-sins#Does God:
Say:
In the Old Testament, we see that God punishes the people of Israel for their sins — they break the law and receive the consequence. And yet we see that no matter how badly they miss the mark, God always welcomes them back.
In his infinite wisdom and grace, God sends his son Jesus to take on the penalty for our sins once and for all.
Jesus and the New Testament teach us not that we are necessarily punished for our sins — but that our sin separates us from God, the God who is the only one who provides abundant life.
We are also taught that God does not bless sin, and that in life after death we will be held accountable. The good news is that the one who will hold us accountable is the same one who gave his life for ours.
The Bible always takes sin seriously, because it destroys life. Out of a response to that great love, we are called to confront sin and evil in our own lives and in the world.
data.in-our-own-lives#In our own lives:
Ask:
What might a faithful response to sin be in our own lives? Confession. Making amends. (Optional resource: Pg 23 in the confirmation notebooks — take turns reading a verse.)
data.in-the-world#In the world:
Ask: What might be a faithful response to sin in the world?
(Optional resource: Pg 27 in the confirmation notebooks — for each listed sin, name the faithful response.)
data.read--exodus-20117-the-ten-commandments#: Open Bibles and read Exodus 20:1–17 together — the Ten Commandments. These are God’s specific articulation of what it looks like to love God and love neighbor.
data.read--matthew-417#Read — Matthew 4:17:
Matthew 4:17 — From that time Jesus began to proclaim, “Repent, for the kingdom of heaven has come near.”
Ask:
What does repent mean? Say:
The Greek word is metanoia — a change of mind, a turn in direction. It doesn’t mean just feeling bad about what you did. It means actually turning around.
data.recap#Recap: Last week we said that sin is missing the mark. Missing the mark at what? Loving God and loving others.

data: Part A of Session 4 · Lord.
data.alternate-track--elijah-on-mount-carmel#:
Source material includes an alternate / extended passage for the which Lord will you follow? question: 1 Kings 18:20–39 (Elijah vs. the prophets of Baal).
Introduce the reading:
King Ahab was the worst king of Israel. He and his wife, Jezebel, imposed the worship of Baal (Baal means “lord”) on the people. Baal was a Canaanite nature god. The question for the people of God: which lord would they serve — Baal or YHWH? The story continues as King Ahab brought 450 prophets of Baal against Elijah, the one prophet of YHWH. It was an epic showdown. This wasn’t some typical disagreement over religion — King Ahab was actually killing off the prophets of God, essentially forcing people to worship Baal.
Have people take turns reading 1 Kings 18:20–39 with emotional inflections: Elijah, prophets of Baal, the crowd, the narrator.
Ask:
Listen for what you think is the most important word / phrase / image / object / event in the passage. We’ll explain our choices afterward. Why was it so important for the people to decide which Lord to follow? Look back at the first commandment — Exodus 20:2–3 — “I am the Lord your God, who brought you out of Egypt, out of the land of slavery. You shall have no other gods before me.” Our lives are defined by our Lord.
