data.big-group--what-does-lord-mean#Big Group:
Recap the table answers.
Ask: What does Lord mean?
Someone or something having power, authority, or influence; a master or ruler.
Big question for the next two weeks: Who is your Lord?
The next two lessons are about where our ultimate loyalties lie — and how that impacts the way we live.
data.competition--jesus-as-lord-is-______#Com:
Say:
We know God is the all-powerful Lord. But just what kind of Lord is he? Well — Jesus shows us.
Split into groups and divvy up the Bible passages from the appendix. Each group reads their passages together.
Say:
This is a competition. We’re going to go from table to table asking you to fill in this statement: “Jesus as Lord is ______.” You have about five minutes to look at the scriptures and come up with as many answers as you can. Write them down.
After five minutes, go in turn to each table — no repeats. Winner is the last one standing.
Read together to close:
John 10:10 — “I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full.”
Say: This is the kind of Lord that Jesus is.
data.family-challenge#Family Challenge:
Before we pray out, a family challenge:
Choose one thing you’ll do over the next week to declare that Jesus is Lord of your family. Examples: pray before bed or a meal; set aside the phone or TV for an hour to pray and spend quality time with family; go to church together; read the Bible together (maybe Exodus 3?!); sing a worship song together; read a Psalm together in the morning.
If your family is here, work with them. If not, think about it — and take it home to them.
data.opening--at-tables#Opening — At Tables: Who is your favorite sports team? (If you don’t have one: favorite artist.) Why are they your favorite? What would it take to make you switch your loyalty to their biggest rival? What if you moved to a different state — would you change your loyalties? Why or why not?
data.prayer--turning-out#Prayer — Turning Out:
Get everyone in a circle. Cross your right arm over your left and take the hand of the person on either side.
Ask each person to say a word that describes Jesus Christ.
Then pray:
Lord Jesus, you are all of these and more. Help us to follow your ways more closely as we leave this place. Amen.
Ask participants to keep their hands joined as they raise their right arms and move under them — creating a circle of joined hands with everyone now facing outward.
Say to the group:
When we turn to Jesus, we turn to the world he loves. Share the good news.
data.read--exodus-31315-the-burning-bush#Read:
Have people take turns reading with emotional inflections: narrator, Moses, God.
Introduce the reading:
Moses has fled Egypt and is living in Midian. He’s married, looking after the flocks of his father-in-law, Jethro. In search of fresh pasture, Moses arrives at Mount Horeb — “the mountain of God.” In this holy place, God speaks to him through a burning bush that will not be consumed by fire. In this encounter, God’s name is revealed to Moses.
Exodus 3:13–15 — Moses said to God, “Suppose I go to the Israelites and say to them, ‘The God of your fathers has sent me to you,’ and they ask me, ‘What is his name?’ Then what shall I tell them?”
God said to Moses, “I am who I am. This is what you are to say to the Israelites: ‘I am has sent me to you.’”
God also said to Moses, “Say to the Israelites, ‘The Lord, the God of your fathers — the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob — has sent me to you.’ This is my name forever, the name you shall call me from generation to generation.”
Ask:
What is God’s name according to v.14? (In Hebrew this is Yahweh*.)* What do you think this name means? Say:
“I AM” is the ultimate statement of self-sufficiency and being ever-present. God is able to do what he wills to do and accomplish what he wills to accomplish. He is utterly dependable, present, and powerful. In other words: God is the Lord of all.
data.the-other-lords#The Other Lords:
Say:
The reality is: it’s not if we’ll have a Lord. It’s who or what will be our Lord. There are many things that try to have authority, power, and influence over us — that try to claim the ultimate lordship over our lives. Some aren’t necessarily bad; some are more sinister. But none of them deserve the number-one spot.
The Scriptures name and warn us about these things often. Here’s an example from the Apostle Paul’s letter to the church in Philippi:
Philippians 3:19 — “Their end is destruction, their god is the belly, and their glory is in their shame; their minds are set on earthly things.”
Ask at tables: What other things might try to have lordship over our lives? (Remember: Lord = someone or something having power, authority, or influence; a master or ruler.)
(Possible answers: grades, work, productivity, power, money, possessions, popularity, social media, video games, sports, relationships, friends, food.)
Say:
Compare these with the descriptions you came up with for Jesus as Lord. Notice the contrast — but don’t knock those things entirely.
Jesus as Lord is full of grace and truth, holy, seeks to do justice, cares for the poor, is forgiving, compassionate, powerful, healing, loving, wise, humble, has all authority, desires to give us rest, and immeasurably more.
Those other things aren’t necessarily all bad. But only when we give Jesus the top spot do we experience the fullest life there is.
John 10:10 — “I have come that they may have life, and have it to the full.”
Say:
Being a follower of Jesus means declaring the bold statement that Jesus is Lord of our lives. Next week we’ll discuss what this means for how we live.

Introduce the reading:
(Optional: post the Books of the New Testament infographic and ask a volunteer to find “Philippians” and note who wrote it.)
The letter to the church in Philippi was written by the Apostle Paul about 20 to 30 years after Jesus lived, and it contains an early hymn to Jesus as Lord.
In Rome, the emperors were considered to be gods. Romans required all the peoples they conquered not only to honor their emperor but also to worship him as a god and call him “Lord.” That was a line neither Jews nor Christians were willing to cross. They would not call Caesar “Lord,” and they would not bow down to worship him.
Show the picture at https://bit.ly/2jTWojp. These ruins are what is left of a fountain in the city of Ephesus. The fountain was built in the second century to honor Roman Emperor Trajan. It included a huge statue of Trajan. These words were inscribed on it: “I am the ruler of the world. The world is under my foot.” Ironically, today only Trajan’s foot and a globe remain.
(If you have the photo of your leader visiting Ephesus on hand, show it here for a laugh.)
Say:
There is only one ruler of the world.
Open Bibles and get three volunteers to read Philippians 2:1–13 — verses 1–4, 5–11, 12–13.
Philippians 2:5–11 — In your relationships with one another, have the same mindset as Christ Jesus: who, being in very nature God, did not consider equality with God something to be used to his own advantage; rather, he made himself nothing by taking the very nature of a servant, being made in human likeness. And being found in appearance as a man, he humbled himself by becoming obedient to death — even death on a cross!
Therefore God exalted him to the highest place and gave him the name that is above every name, that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father.
Ask:
Given that the Romans demanded that all people call the emperor “Lord,” what kind of fallout would come to Christians for calling Jesus “Lord”? (Persecuted. Laughed at. Not included in social circles. People thinking they were weird. Not getting jobs or opportunities.) If they were going to face these consequences, why would they call Jesus their Lord? (Because he brings them abundant life.) If Jesus is Lord, how is he different from so many rulers of the world? (Hint: v.5–11. He doesn’t use his power for his own gain but for the gain of others.) Should honoring Jesus as Lord — the highest authority of our lives — make a difference in how we act or what we say?
data.scenarios#Scenarios:
Break students into four groups of 3–4. Each group gets one scenario. Have them come up with two responses — one if Jesus is Lord, one if Jesus is not — and act them out after a few minutes.
Scenarios:
Someone is talking badly about someone else in the locker room. The person next to you in class offers to give you the answers to the quiz. There are dishes in the sink when you get home from school. You are invited to go to the soup kitchen to feed people who are homeless Saturday morning.
data.the-big-question#The Big Question:
Say:
The big question this unit is “Who is your Lord?” You don’t have to answer out loud — but sit with these two questions:
Are you ready to claim Jesus Christ as your Lord? Why or why not? Are you ready to live in a way that shows loyalty to your Lord? Why or why not?
data.the-mind-of-christ#The Mind of Christ:
Ask for a volunteer who can draw. On the whiteboard, draw a large outline of a human head — large enough to fill the board.
Call on the youth to draw inside the head any symbols, words, or images that signify having the same mind as Christ. These could be Jesus’ attitudes, actions, or characteristics — things Paul calls Christians to imitate.
Ask: Who is someone you know that has the mindset of Christ? Why?
Pass out note cards and pens. Have students consider which particular qualities they would like to practice in their own lives and write them down.
Ask:
Would anyone share some of the qualities you wrote down? Is it easy to live up to these qualities? Accountability is helping intentions become reality. What are some ways we can hold each other accountable? What are some ways we can celebrate when we see one another having the mind of Christ?

data: Part A of Session 5 · Salvation.
data.before-class#Before class: Confirmation ground rules; ask who will open in prayer next week Whiteboard + markers Phones or laptops for the Blooket game A guest lined up to share their salvation testimony (about 10 minutes)
data.close#Close: Close with a popcorn prayer — each person prays one short sentence or word out loud, popping up as they feel led.
data.game--blooket#Game — Blooket:
Play a Blooket set on “things that claim to save us.” Ours is here (use the code when prompted):
https://dashboard.blooket.com/set/655bb11da92a368a39ff7f5a Ask:
Look at the advertisements, products, and institutions on the board. Why do these places look for problems people have and make promises to fix them?
data.icebreaker#Icebreaker:
Ask:
What is your favorite thing about Thanksgiving? Do you have any family Thanksgiving traditions? What is your least favorite thing about Thanksgiving?
data.testimony#Testimony: Introduce the guest who will share their salvation story. After they finish, open it up for questions.
data.the-big-question#The Big Question:
Write on the board: “Who is your Savior?”
These next two weeks, we’re talking about this question.
data.the-presbyterian-promise--grace#The Pres:
Ask: In the Presbyterian religion, what is our promise?
Say:
Our promise is GRACE. Grace fully known in the love and sacrifice of Jesus.
Write this on the board:
G od’s R iches A t C hrist’s E xpense
Say:
Presbyterians believe that God promises a solution to our brokenness and the brokenness of the world in the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ. We call that salvation.
Salvation is God’s gift — to save us from all that is against God, and to give us abundant life here and now, and in eternity.
data.what-is-a-savior#What Is a Savior?:
Have students google the word Savior.
You’re likely to see two definitions:
A person who saves someone or something (especially a country or cause) from danger, and who is regarded with the veneration of a religious figure. Synonyms: rescuer, liberator, deliverer, emancipator, champion, knight in shining armor, friend in need, good Samaritan, salvation. (In Christianity) God or Jesus Christ as the redeemer of sin and saver of souls. Synonyms: Christ, Jesus, Jesus Christ, Redeemer, Messiah, Lord. Ask: So — what do we need to be saved from?

data: Part B of Session 5 · Salvation.
data.before-class#Before class: Confirmation ground rules; ask who will open in prayer next week Bibles for all Whiteboard + markers Screen / laptop for YouTube videos Index cards with the three passage groups written on them (see Breakout Groups below)
data.breakout-groups--salvation-in-three-keys: Break into groups. Give each group an index card with a salvation theme + passages and the question set. Each group reads, discusses, and presents.
data.common-thread#Common Thread:
After groups present, discuss: what’s the common theme across all three keys?
Ask: What questions do you still have about salvation?
data.group-1--salvation-means-freedom#Group 1:
Read Exodus 3:1–10:
What did Moses and the Hebrew people need to be saved (freed) from? In what ways is this a story of salvation? What does the story tell us about the heart of God? Read Luke 19:1–10 (Zacchaeus):
What did Zacchaeus need to be saved (freed) from? In what ways is this a story of salvation? What does the story tell us about the heart of God?
data.group-2--salvation-means-healing-and-who:
Read 2 Kings 5:1–15 (Naaman):
What did Naaman need to be saved (healed) from? In what ways is this a story of salvation? What does the story tell us about the heart of God? Read Mark 2:1–12 (the man lowered through the roof):
What did the man who was paralyzed need to be saved (healed) from? In what ways is this a story of salvation? What does the story tell us about the heart of God?
data.group-3--salvation-means-forgiveness#Gro:
Read Matthew 18:21–35 (the unmerciful servant):
What did the men who were in debt need to be saved (forgiven) from? In what ways is this a story of salvation? What does the story tell us about the heart of God? Read Luke 15:11–32 (the prodigal son):
What did the prodigal need to be saved (forgiven) from? In what ways is this a story of salvation? What does the story tell us about the heart of God?
data.opening#Opening:
Ask:
How was everyone’s Christmas? What is one Christmas tradition your family has?
data.optional--humanity-and-divinity-of-chris:
If there’s time: get into a discussion about Christ’s humanity and Christ’s divinity.
Draw a continuum on the board. Ask confirmands to name things that fall on either side.
Say:
As fully human, Christ gets us and understands our struggle. As fully God, Christ has the power to save us from sin in the world.
data.read--ephesians-2#Read — Ephesians 2:
Hand out Bibles. Have the confirmands turn to Ephesians 2.
Ask a volunteer to read aloud verses 1–3.
Ask:
Why does Paul tell readers they were dead? What kind of death is Paul talking about? Ask a volunteer to read aloud verses 4–7.
Ask:
How does God change people in Christ, and why? Out of love, God saves people, makes them alive with Christ, and restores relationship with God through Christ. Ask a volunteer to read aloud verses 8–10.
Ask:
What do humans need to do to receive God’s saving love? Based on these verses, what do you think grace means? If our good works cannot save us, why do them?
data.recap#Recap:
Ask: What does Savior mean? (Invite synonyms; write them on the board.)
Write on the board: “Who is your Savior?”
Say:
The things of the world all promise to give us a solution to different problems.
Ask: In the Presbyterian religion, what is our promise? (Wait for answers.)
Say:
Our promise is GRACE. Grace fully known in the love and sacrifice of Jesus.
Write on the board:
God’s Riches At Christ’s Expense
Say:
Presbyterians believe that God promises a solution to our brokenness and the brokenness of the world in the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ. We call that salvation. Salvation is God’s gift to save us from all that is against God and give us abundant life here and now, and in eternity.
data.salvation-is-bigger-than-heaven-and-hell:
Say:
Considering our eternal destiny is only one aspect of salvation. The Bible says a wide variety of things about that subject, but we can break it down into three:
The Bible says that God chooses those who are saved. The Bible says that when we choose God, we are saved. The Bible says that all will be saved. Say:
Those things live in tension — and we are called to embrace the mystery.
What we know for sure is that salvation comes through Christ, and that we experience salvation through our relationship with him. But the idea of salvation in the Bible is bigger and broader than just thinking about heaven or hell.
data.video#Video:
Watch one or both of the following and ask afterward: “How would you say salvation is described in that video?”
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e-71IaDiUkY https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fADMgCVOSvs

data: Part A of Session 6 · Disciple.
data.before-class#Before class: Confirmation ground rules Bibles for all Things to make a cross with — sticks, twine, popsicle sticks, whatever’s on hand A downstairs / outdoor space to move to for the cross-making activity Big question for the next two weeks: What does it mean to be a disciple of Jesus?
data.cross-activity#Cross Activity:
Move downstairs (or outside). Make crosses together. As you work, consider Jesus’ call to take up your own cross — to lay down your life for the sake of others.
Ask:
What were you thinking of as you constructed your cross? How did you feel — how do you feel — about the call to discipleship?
data.opening#Opening:
Ask: Would you say that being a Christian is safe or risky? Why?
(Teacher: give a personal story about a time your faith cost you something.)
Ask: Sometimes we confuse belief and discipleship. Does anyone know the difference?
Say:
Belief implies something inward. Discipleship implies something active — a change in one’s daily life.
Ask: What is the central symbol of Christianity?
(The cross.)
Say:
Following Jesus is not an easy thing. It means taking up the cross.
data.read--mark-83138#Read — Mark 8:31–38:
Introduce the reading:
In Jesus’ time, people expected God to send a Messiah — Christ, “anointed one.” Most people assumed the Messiah would lead the people of Israel in a nationalistic revolution against Rome. Peter thought Jesus would be that kind of Messiah. So when Jesus says that he must suffer, be rejected and killed, and after three days rise again — Peter reacts.
Ask a volunteer to read aloud Mark 8:31–33.
Ask:
Why do you think Peter rebukes (reprimands, criticizes) Jesus? Why does Jesus tell Peter to get behind him? Disciples follow Jesus and his teachings — not the other way around. Ask another volunteer to read aloud Mark 8:34–38.
Ask:
What do you think Jesus means by “take up their cross” (v.34)? Disciples should be prepared to face sacrifice and challenges like Jesus’ own life and death — when their beliefs and values conflict with those of worldly authorities. How do you think Jesus’ disciples reacted to his call to take up their cross? What does it look like to lose one’s life for Jesus’ sake and for the sake of the gospel (v.35)?

data: Part B of Session 6 · Disciple.
data.before-class#Before class: Confirmation ground rules Church bulletins (one per student) Note cards, pens Whiteboard + markers Big question continues: What does it mean to be a disciple of Jesus?
data.commitment--i-will-follow-jesus#Commitme:
Write the following phrase on the board:
“I will follow Jesus. Even though following him might put me at risk, I want to stand up for ______________.”
Look back at your notecard and choose a situation (or make a new one) where you want to stand for the right thing.
One at a time, invite students to stand and say: “I will follow Jesus. Even though following him might put me at risk, I want to stand up for ____________” — naming a person, cause, or ministry.
Prompt the group’s response: “I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me.” (Philippians 4:13)
(Teacher: be prepared to stand and speak first to prompt others.)
When everyone is standing, join hands as the church and pray together for the strength to follow Jesus in your daily lives.
Tell confirmands you will pray for them this week and invite them to pray for you.
data.looking-ahead--your-own-confirmation#Loo:
Say:
We follow Jesus together. Being a disciple of Jesus is about training together in the sacrificial love of Jesus. As we look ahead to confirmation:
Ask:
What sacrifices do you think you might be asked to make because you are being confirmed?
data.notecards--courage-to-stand-firm#Notecar:
Pass out note cards and pens. Ask confirmands to write down two situations in which it would take courage to stand firm in their faith and stay true to Jesus’ teachings.
Write these possibilities on the board to get them thinking:
Seeing someone being bullied Being with friends who are gossiping about others Being around someone who says people who believe in God are dumb Choosing to honor commitments at church when others are not Observing family members wasting resources or doing something harmful Seeing a friend who is a Christian being hateful toward someone else Ask:
What do you risk sacrificing by showing courage and standing firm? What good things might happen if you do?
data.read--acts-75160-stephen-the-first-marty:
Say:
Acts is a history of the early church, written by Luke. Stephen is the first martyr — he stood up for his faith regardless of the consequences.
Ask a volunteer to read aloud Acts 7:51–60.
Ask:
Are there ways in which you stand up for your faith at home or at school? What is difficult about standing up for your faith? What does this story tell us about discipleship? Say:
Following Jesus is rewarding — but it is also risky. Christ doesn’t call us to play it safe. He calls us to sacrifice and to stand up for our faith.
Ask:
How do you think Christian discipleship (denying self and taking up the cross) compares to loving God, neighbors, and self with mind, soul, and heart — in other words, with everything you have? How are they different?
data.read--mark-124144-the-generous-widow#Rea:
Ask a volunteer to read aloud.
Ask:
Why was the woman’s gift worth more than all those who put in a lot more money than she did? What does this story tell us about discipleship?
data.read--matthew-202028-the-greatest-in-the:
Ask a volunteer to read aloud.
Ask:
What kind of kingdom do you think the mother of John and James thought Jesus was going to have? Why do you think the others were angry? What does this story tell us about discipleship?
data.recap#Recap:
Ask: Who remembers what it means to be a disciple?
To take up your cross and follow Jesus. To make sacrifices for the sake of God and others.
data.worship-as-the-gymnasium-of-discipleship:
Hand out copies of your church’s worship service bulletin. Write the components on the board.
Say:
Sunday worship is like a gymnasium — it’s meant to train us to be disciples of Christ throughout the week.
Ask:
What is your favorite part? Which parts of the service require responses from us? Where does the worship service call on church members to deny themselves? To give sacrificially? What, if anything, would you change in our worship so that sacrificially following Jesus was emphasized more?

data: Part A of Session 7 · Love.
data.application--four-ways-to-love#Applicati:
At tables, have each person name:
One way you can show phileo at home. One way you can show phileo outside of the home (at work, school, somewhere else). One way you can show agape at home. One way you can show agape outside of the home.
data.ask--at-tables#Ask — at tables: Why do you think Jesus asked Simon Peter if he loved him? Jesus asked Peter if he loved (agape) him. Why do you think Peter responded, “I love (phileo) you”? If this were the only Bible story we had about love, how would you define Christian love?
data.ask--in-big-group#Ask — in big group: What is the connection between loving Jesus and feeding his sheep? Whom do sheep represent in the story? Who are Jesus’ sheep today? What actions constitute “feeding” them?
data.before-class#Before class:
Confirmation ground rules; invite someone to pray Print copies of Resource 7a (the phileo vs. agape sheet) — enough for everyone or partners Print copies of John 21:15–19 (narrator / Jesus / Peter roles) Mural paper or large whiteboard + markers Big question for the next two weeks:
Will you be Christ’s faithful disciple, obeying his Word and showing his love?
data.close--we-love-because-he-first-loved-us:
1 John 4:19 — “We love because he first loved us.”
Encourage the confirmands to think of ways they are both sheep and feeders — so they see themselves as contributors as well as recipients.
Close in prayer.
data.opener#Opener:
Ask: What does it mean to show Christ’s love?
Brainstorm synonyms for the word love — affection, adore, fondness, tenderness, devotion. Push the group to think more tangibly about acts and experiences of love.
Optional game: Last LOVE standing — go around the room; each person names a kind of love without repeating. Last one standing wins.
data.phileo-vs-agape#Phileo vs. Agape:
Hand out copies of Resource 7a. Review the differences between phileo (brotherly love, mutual affection) and agape (sacrificial love). Work on the sheet together, helping one another translate the words for love in each Bible passage.
Return to the brainstormed love list on the mural paper. Ask participants to choose the synonyms and expressions of love that most closely resemble phileo and agape.
data.read--john-211519#Read — John 21:15–19:
Context: This is after the resurrection. Jesus has appeared to the disciples on the beach. Peter — the one who denied Jesus three times before the crucifixion — is about to be asked a question three times.
Invite volunteers to read the parts of Jesus, Peter, and a narrator.
John 21:15–19 — When they had finished eating, Jesus said to Simon Peter, “Simon son of John, do you love me more than these?”
“Yes, Lord,” he said, “you know that I love you.”
Jesus said, “Feed my lambs.”
Again Jesus said, “Simon son of John, do you love me?”
He answered, “Yes, Lord, you know that I love you.”
Jesus said, “Take care of my sheep.”
The third time he said to him, “Simon son of John, do you love me?”
Peter was hurt because Jesus asked him the third time, “Do you love me?” He said, “Lord, you know all things; you know that I love you.”
Jesus said, “Feed my sheep. Very truly I tell you, when you were younger you dressed yourself and went where you wanted; but when you are old you will stretch out your hands, and someone else will dress you and lead you where you do not want to go.” (Jesus said this to indicate the kind of death by which Peter would glorify God.) Then he said to him, “Follow me!”

data:
Part B of Session 7 · Love.
Get right to it — the campus tour takes longer than you think.
data.activity--food-pantry--food-bags#Activit:
Head to the food pantry and put together food bags.
Invite a leader to share briefly:
How did you get involved? What do you do there? How has it impacted your faith and the way you show Christ’s love? How could we get involved?

data.close#Close: Have students come up with their own ways to love this week — one specific thing, one specific person. Bless them in prayer.
data.discussion--faithful-disciple-showing-hi:
“Will you be Christ’s faithful disciple, … showing his love?”
Ask:
How does doing the work of love help you understand who Jesus Christ is for you? Why do you think sacrificial love (agape) is important for following Jesus and showing his love? Why do you think mutual affection (phileo) is important for following Jesus and showing his love? How will you show agape and phileo to others as a part of the church?
data.recap#Recap:
Ask: What does it mean to show Christ’s love?
Ask: What two types of love showed up in last week’s passage?
Phileo and agape.
Ask: What’s the difference between the two?
Recap John 21:15–19.
Ask: When are you a sheep? When are you a feeder?
data.walking-tour--how-the-church-shows-chris:
Ask: What are the different ways this church shows Christ’s love?
Then take a field trip through the building. Walk the whole campus. At each stop, name what happens there and how it’s an expression of Christ’s love. A template tour (adapt to your church):
Prayer pit / chapel — it all starts with prayer Mission room — mission trips, water walks, partnerships; walk through the things on the walls Music / arts room — music, gifts Kitchen — meals, delivering meals to shut-ins Fellowship hall — giving, youth group Band closet / supply room — food bags for homeless-service partners, band “Man cave” / utility room — mission shoes for Guatemala, middle-school missions, senior care, buildings and grounds Preschool / nursery — Sunday school, preschool Food Pantry — service days (Monday & Wednesday, in our case) Pastoral care / connections office — getting people connected, the good-neighbor fund, volunteer coordination Conference room — committees (admin, HR), prayer meetings, elders, deacons Sanctuary — music, preaching, tech, Ash Wednesday, communion, tithing, weddings, funerals Graveyard — care for those who have passed away Bonus: If Ash Wednesday is coming up, ask who can help with set up and who can read the confession.

data: Part A of Session 8 · Teaching.
data.before-class#Before class:
Confirmation ground rules Note cards + pens Printed Resource 8a (Common English Bible + The Message paraphrases of 2 Timothy 1:9–10) — enough for everyone or partners Bibles The BIG Journal (or equivalent) if your program uses one; page references below assume it Write the big question on the board:
WILL YOU DEVOTE YOURSELF TO THE CHURCH’S TEACHING?
data.close#Close:
Say:
We’ll revisit the Apostles’ Creed and learn more about our own worship service next week.
Close in prayer.
data.i-believe--i-love#I Believe / I Love:
(Follow page 48 in the Teacher’s Guide.)
Direct each person (or pair) to write the words “I believe” at the top of their note card. Have them write four things:
One thing they believe about God One thing they believe about Jesus One thing they believe about the Holy Spirit One thing they believe about the church Now flip the card over (or use a fresh one) and write “I love” at the top. Do the same thing with love instead of belief.
Ask:
Which was the hardest belief for you to write? (Most groups say the Holy Spirit.) Was it easier to name what you believe about God, or what you love about God? Why? Is there a connection between what you believe about someone and what you love about someone? Explain. Pray:
Thank you for all the faithful people who have come before us. Help us to learn more about you. Where we have questions, give us patience and a willingness to listen for answers. Where we are confident, give us patience and a willingness to share our faith in new ways. Give us the willingness to listen to others and to speak honestly. Amen.
data.opener#Opener:
Confirmation ground rules + highs and lows go-around.
Say:
Today we’re going to look at the Apostles’ Creed — a statement of faith that is over a thousand years old, unites multiple denominations of Christianity, and is based in what was professed by those in the early church.
data.paraphrase-exercise#Paraphrase Exercise:
Distribute copies of Resource 8a (CEB + The Message). Ask volunteers to read the verses aloud.
Ask each participant to write a paraphrase of 2 Timothy 1:9–10 (BIG Journal p.74) as either an “I believe” or “I love” statement. Ask volunteers to read their paraphrases to the group.
Ask:
What important things do we learn about what Christians believe from these verses? How might speaking creeds together in worship encourage Christians?
data.read--2-timothy-1#Read — 2 Timothy 1:
Say:
First and Second Timothy and Titus are called the Pastoral Letters (or Pastoral Epistles) in the New Testament. The Apostle Paul — or another church leader writing in Paul’s name — sent these letters to pastors with advice about how to face the challenges of standing firm in the faith.
Distribute Bibles. Invite volunteers to read Acts 16:1–2 and 1 Thessalonians 3:2, 5–6 to learn a bit about Timothy.
Have participants turn to 2 Timothy 1. Ask a volunteer to read aloud verses 1–14.
Ask:
What is the problem Paul is addressing in the letter? Why do you think Paul mentions Timothy’s mother and grandmother? (v.5) What is the sound teaching Paul is referring to? Why is it important that Christians “hold to the standard of sound teaching” (v.13)? Which of Paul’s words to Timothy do you find most encouraging? Why? To help participants understand v.6, ask a volunteer to find and read aloud Acts 13:2–3. Point out that the Spirit was given to Paul through the laying on of hands, and Paul gave this gift to Timothy.
Have participants read aloud 2 Timothy 1:9–10 in unison. Tell them scholars think these verses quote an early Christian creed — words of faith that early Christians recited in worship.
Ask: What word or phrase from these verses grabs your attention the most? Why?
data.the-apostles-creed#The Apostles\u0026rsq:
Say:
A creed helps us describe what we affirm as Christians. The word creed has the connotation of “I give my heart” and is often translated as “I believe.”
The Apostles’ Creed was developed over a few hundred years after Jesus lived. It’s based on Scripture and the understanding and experience of the earliest Christians.
Parallel example:
Centuries after the Constitution was written, Martin Luther King Jr. argued for abolishing segregation based on the Declaration of Independence (all men are created equal). He appealed to a document everyone held to be authoritative in how this country should run. The Apostles’ Creed is our version of that — a document the church has agreed names what it stands on.
Read the Apostles’ Creed aloud (BIG Journal p.72–73). A printable copy: https://www.pcabookstore.com/samples/11589.pdf
Ask:
Why is it important to notice that the creed begins with the words “I believe in…”?

data.before-class#Before class:
Confirmation ground rules Note cards + pens Apostles’ Creed matching cards (kept in the confirmation/retreat room). Enough to pair students; otherwise two-sided copies cut from the teacher’s guide. Collect these in bunches afterward for next year. Smartphones or tablets for students to look up symbols Copies of your church’s worship bulletin (one per student) Whiteboard + markers Write the big question on the board again:
WILL YOU DEVOTE YOURSELF TO THE CHURCH’S TEACHING?
data.close#Close:
Read the Apostles’ Creed as a closing and pray:
Holy God, we praise you for the example of the heroes of faith who have run this race before us. Give us strength and perseverance, and help us fix our eyes on Jesus, in whose name we pray. Amen.
data.isaiah-6-as-the-shape-of-worship#Isaiah:
Isaiah 6:1 — “In the year that King Uzziah died, I saw the Lord sitting on a throne, high and lofty, and the hem of his robe filled the temple.”
Worship begins with God.
v.2–3 — “Seraphs were in attendance above him; each had six wings… And one called to another and said, ‘Holy, holy, holy is the Lord of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory.’”
The call to worship — affirming God’s goodness and holiness.
v.4–5 — “The pivots on the thresholds shook at the voices of those who called, and the house filled with smoke. And I said, ‘Woe is me! I am lost, for I am a man of unclean lips, and I live among a people of unclean lips, yet my eyes have seen the King, the Lord of hosts!’”
Confession — in response to God’s holiness and our sin.
v.6–7 — “Then one of the seraphs flew to me, holding a live coal… The seraph touched my mouth with it and said, ‘Now that this has touched your lips, your guilt has departed and your sin is blotted out.’”
Assurance of pardon.
v.8a — “Then I heard the voice of the Lord saying, ‘Whom shall I send, and who will go for us?’”
Sermon / Scripture — the word of God.
v.8b — “And I said, ‘Here am I; send me!’”
Response / offering — sending.
data.opener#Opener:
Confirmation ground rules.
Say:
Back to the Apostles’ Creed — the summary of our faith that has been the central expression of the church’s teaching from the beginning.
Read the creed aloud together.
data.optional--the-barmen-declaration-and-the:
If your group has older or more curious confirmands, split into two groups (one teacher each). One goes over the Barmen Declaration (Nazi Germany, 1934); the other goes over the Confession of Belhar (apartheid South Africa, 1986). The Confession of 1967 (civil rights, nuclear war) can substitute or supplement.
Hand out copies of Resource 8f. Let them use phones to look up the historical background.
Discuss:
What is required of a church to take a stand in times of crisis? How can a faith statement that takes a stand create change for good in the world? For whom or what would you take a stand in the middle of the cafeteria? In the middle of the congregation? Against what evil or injustice in the world would you write a faith statement?
data.optional--where-did-the-bible-come-from#:
If you have extra time or the class has questions about scripture, the source material includes an extended teaching on why we trust the Bible, including:
How the canon of the New Testament was formed (through the fourth century) Apostolic authority and its transmission through the bishops Why the “telephone game” argument against Scripture is weak — the care taken by scribes, the volume of manuscripts, the precision vs. other ancient texts Why the “Christianity was invented to keep people in line” argument is historically inaccurate — Pliny the Younger’s 113 AD letter to Trajan describing execution of Christians, and why early Christians had nothing to gain from inventing the faith
data.symbols-of-the-creed#Symbols of the Cree:
Pass out the symbol cards. Have the group research the meaning of the symbols on their phones or tablets.
Challenge: draw symbols for the phrases that aren’t on the cards:
“Maker of heaven and earth” “He descended into hell” “The quick and the dead” “The communion of saints” Add these to the symbol cards.
Consider putting them in pairs. Have them try to put the creed in order just looking at the symbols.
data.the-cloud-of-witnesses#The Cloud of Witn:
Say:
The phrase “the communion of saints” in the Apostles’ Creed refers to the relationships among Christians past, present, and future. The New Testament letter called Hebrews describes this communion as a “cloud of witnesses.” The cloud refers to all the saints who have gone before us as our encouragers in the faith — like our great forebears Abraham and Sarah, or the ones who are great only for us, like our parents and teachers in the faith.
Invite a volunteer to read aloud Hebrews 12:1–2.
Ask:
What images of racing did you notice? The “cloud of witnesses” — the runners who have already finished the race and are cheering on those still competing, identified by name in Hebrews 11. “Lay aside every weight” — runners of that time ran with little or no clothing, in order to run faster. The runner keeping their eyes fixed on the goal, which is Jesus Christ. What words of encouragement did you hear? Why might the people who first heard this Scripture have needed words of encouragement? Why do we need encouragement?
data.worship-as-trained-discipleship#Worship:
Hand out copies of your church’s worship service bulletin. Write the main components on the board.
Say:
Sunday worship is like a gymnasium — it’s meant to train and teach us to be disciples of Christ throughout the week.
Ask:
What is your favorite part? Which parts of the service require responses from us? What, if anything, would you change in our worship so that sacrificially following Jesus was emphasized? Work through the components of the service and talk about what they’re teaching us — what they’re inviting us into.
A useful frame: Isaiah 6. The structure of Christian worship grows out of Isaiah’s vision of the throne room.

data: Part A of Session 9 · Fellowship.
data.before-class#Before class: Confirmation ground rules; choose someone to pray Paper and pens for every student Bibles
data.close#Close:
Leader: Through Baptism, we become members of the body of Christ, therefore…
All: Christ has no body but yours, no hands, no feet on earth but yours, yours are the eyes with which he looks compassion on this world, yours are the feet with which he walks to do good, yours are the hands, with which he blesses all the world. Yours are the hands, yours are the feet, yours are the eyes, you are his body. Christ has no body now but yours, no hands, no feet on earth but yours, yours are the eyes with which he looks compassion on this world. Christ has no body now on earth but yours.
(Attributed to Teresa of Ávila.)
data.first--gathering#First — Gathering:
Ask a volunteer to read Hebrews 10:24–25:
And let us consider how we may spur one another on toward love and good deeds, not giving up meeting together, as some are in the habit of doing, but encouraging one another — and all the more as you see the Day approaching.
Answer in your group, or as a family:
What is one of your favorite memories gathering at a church function? In what ways can your family gather with the people of God? (Church on Sunday, dinner / prayer with other Christian families, events like Pancake Dinner, mission trips, etc.) Why might you need to encourage one another when it comes to continuing to gather? What are some spaces you want to commit to gathering in after this confirmation class is over? (Church, Sunday School, youth group, retreats, etc.)
data.naming-gifts#Naming Gifts:
Say:
One of the ways we love in this way is by sharing our gifts with one another. Scripture tells us that we’ve all been given gifts by God to join God at work in the world — loving the world.
As we close, we want to allow others to identify gifts they might see in us.
Ask each confirmand to write their name at the top of a page, then pass the page to the person on the right. Notice the name of the person at the top of the page — it’s okay if you don’t know them that well. Have everyone write (legibly) a gift or talent they think that person has.
(Share a list of sample gifts / talents to prime the pump.)
Pass the papers around the circle. When everyone’s page is full, have each student read their own page aloud. Prompt this response:
Leader: “Thank you, God, for NAME who brings us the gift of GIFT.”
Group: “Thank you, God, for good gifts.”
data.opener--koinonia#Opener — Koinonia:
Ask: Can anyone guess what this Greek word means?
Koinonia — Fellowship.
Big question: Will you devote yourself to the church’s fellowship?
Say:
Koinonia expresses the idea of sharing in a common bond. Action is always included in its meaning — it implies contribution, sharing, and participation. Fellowship is not just being together; it’s doing together.
Doing life together. Repeat after me: Doing life together.
data.second--love#Second — Love:
Introduce the reading:
This story takes place while Jesus and his disciples are eating the Passover dinner — what people sometimes call the Last Supper. In Jesus’ day, most people traveled on foot wearing sandals. Their feet quickly got very dusty. In well-to-do households, a servant would wash a visitor’s feet when they entered. This job was considered the lowliest of all the servant tasks. In this encounter, Jesus offers the disciples a vivid example of how they are to be a loving community based on his example.
Read John 13:3–5, 14–15:
Jesus, knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands, and that he had come from God and was going to God, got up from the table, took off his outer robe, and tied a towel around himself. Then he poured water into a basin and began to wash the disciples’ feet and to wipe them with the towel that was tied around him… “So if I, your Lord and Teacher, have washed your feet, you also ought to wash one another’s feet. For I have set you an example, that you also should do as I have done to you.”
Ask (in small groups if you have time; otherwise big group):
What would be like foot-washing today? (Washing the dishes? Scrubbing the bathroom? Cleaning up someone else’s mess at a church supper? Spending time with a lonely or demanding person at lunch?) Say:
Refer back to agape — sacrificial love. It takes action.
Ask:
When do you ever see followers of Jesus washing others’ feet? Read:
John 13:35 — “By this everyone will know that you are my disciples, if you have love for one another.”
Say: Jesus washed even Judas’s feet.
data.the-priesthood-of-all-believers#The Prie:
Say:
We want to begin by talking about what the Presbyterian church calls the priesthood of all believers.
Ask: Any guesses as to what this means?
Read — 1 Peter 2:4–5:
Come to him, a living stone, though rejected by mortals yet chosen and precious in God’s sight, and like living stones, let yourselves be built into a spiritual house, to be a holy priesthood, to offer spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ.
Say:
The priesthood of all believers asserts that all humans have access to God through Christ, the true high priest, and thus do not need a priestly mediator.
Priests acted as the middle person between us and God. Priests of this nature are still used in the Catholic and Orthodox traditions, but when the notion of the priesthood of all believers was introduced in the Reformation, it made the claim that all Christians were equal.
The priesthood of all believers also signifies that all members of the body of Christ can speak and contribute to the life of the church.
So, as we are called to fellowship — koinonia, doing life together — it’s a fellowship as the priesthood of all believers, where we all have equal access to God.
data.the-two-marks-of-koinonia-gathering-and-:
Say:
The fellowship — koinonia — of the church is ultimately marked by two things: gathering and love.

data: Part B of Session 9 · Fellowship.
data.before-class#Before class: Confirmation ground rules; choose someone to pray Whiteboard + markers Note cards for new metaphors Pens Printed reading-closing handouts for everyone Children & Youth Sunday template (see events/children-and-youth-sunday)
data.body-parts-debate#Body Parts Debate:
Have a volunteer draw a simple sketch of each of the following on the whiteboard: hand, foot, head, eyes, ears, nose.
Ask participants to write and / or draw the important things done by that part of the body.
Then invite volunteers to pretend to be a body part in a skit, two at a time. Have them argue with one another over who is the most important part of the body.
20 seconds to say why they are the better body part 20 seconds for the opposing body part 10-second rebuttals each Do 3–4 different match-ups.
data.close#Close:
Read the Teresa of Ávila closing aloud together:
Leader: Through Baptism, we become members of the body of Christ, therefore…
All: Christ has no body but yours, no hands, no feet on earth but yours, yours are the eyes with which he looks compassion on this world, yours are the feet with which he walks to do good, yours are the hands, with which he blesses all the world. Yours are the hands, yours are the feet, yours are the eyes, you are his body. Christ has no body now but yours, no hands, no feet on earth but yours, yours are the eyes with which he looks compassion on this world. Christ has no body now on earth but yours.
data.gifts-inventory#Gifts Inventory:
Have youth open BIG Journal to pages 82–83. Read the instructions together, then allow a few quiet moments for confirmands to mark their responses.
After they finish, discuss:
How did you feel as you completed the gifts inventory? What stands out for you about your list? How did you feel last week when people spoke about your gifts? In what ways can your gifts contribute to the unity and mission of the church? (Mission = to restore the world through sacrificial love.)
data.planning-children--youth-sunday#Planning:
Go over the upcoming Children & Youth Sunday service. These are the ways this year’s class can share their gifts:
Welcome / announcements — 2 students Sermon — one 10-minute or two 5-minute messages Possible topics: journey of faith; Deuteronomy 26 (“a wandering Aramean was my father”) with a recitation of the history of God’s people; the Road to Emmaus Scripture reader — student Tech team — video, slides, sound (3 students) Greeters — 3 students (youth / children) Reference materials on the Children & Youth Sunday page: welcome template, student sermon, student scriptures, and past program samples.
data.read--1-corinthians-121227#Read — 1 Cori:
Introduce:
The church in Corinth was having trouble remembering the koinonia — the fellowship — that God intended for them. So Paul used this analogy.
Read 1 Corinthians 12:12–27.
Discuss:
According to the passage, why does the body need to be made up of different members? (To contribute something unique and help the whole function — v.17.) If we are one body, what does that mean about the church as a community? (Interdependent. We’re all connected and need each other. There is no following Jesus apart from the church.) “There is no following Jesus apart from the church.” What might that mean? What are our responsibilities to one another? Say:
The Spirit gives us all gifts to use so that the body of Christ can function. God has gifted each member of the church for some form of ministry — of performing sacrificial love. Because the gift is for us, it is personal. Because the gift is for God’s world, it is communal.
data.recap#Recap:
Big question: Will you devote yourself to the church’s fellowship?
Ask: Does anyone remember what koinonia means?
Fellowship. Sharing in a common bond. Action is always included — contribution, sharing, participation. Not just being together, but doing together. Doing life together.
data.rewrite-the-metaphor#Rewrite the Metapho:
Have the youth rewrite 1 Corinthians 12:12–27 using a new metaphor. Invite them to choose one: a tree, a machine, a symphony orchestra, a sports team. For example:
“For just as the orchestra is one and has many musicians, and all the musicians of the orchestra, though many, are one orchestra, so it is with Christ.”
Share a few out loud.

data:
Session 10 · Breaking Bread.
Teach this one over a shared meal, off-campus if possible. Ours is at a local diner (Woody’s). The theology lands differently when the bread on the table is literal. Big question: Will you devote yourself to the breaking of bread?
data.close#Close: If your context allows, consider sharing communion together at the table before you leave. Pray for the class and for the church’s life to be marked by this meal.
data.conversation-questions#Conversation Ques:
A short, conversational lesson. Walk the table through each question and invite answers.
Why do you think we celebrate communion?
To come together in unity Jesus told us to Renewing our covenant with God
data.future-implications#Future Implications:
Ask: What does communion point us toward in the future?
We anticipate the great banquet table of Christ in heaven — where every tear is wiped away and all things are made new.
data.past-implications#Past Implications:
Ask: What does communion connect us to in the past?
We remember Jesus’ sacrifice for us and our covenant with God.
data.present-implications#Present Implication:
Ask: What happens in the present when we take communion?
We encounter the Holy Spirit through the eating of the bread and the drinking of the juice.
data.the-elements#The Elements:
Ask: What does the bread stand for?
Jesus’ body, broken for us in his sacrifice on the cross. Ask: What does the juice / wine stand for?
Jesus’ blood, shed for us for the forgiveness of sins.
data.the-table#The Table:
Ask: How big is Jesus’ table?
Big enough for everyone. Ask: Why is it important that we break bread together?
We follow Jesus together. To remember that we are a unified body. To enjoy one another. To nurture our souls and our bodies.

data:
Part A of Session 11 · Prayer.
Focus: Prayer focuses our attention on God and away from ourselves. When that happens, we open our hearts to God to become active in our lives, seeking community and justice for all humanity.
data.before-class#Before class: Lord’s Prayer + scripture sheets printed for every student Molding clay — one piece per student A cup or bowl of water per student, plus paper towels Bibles Big question: Will you devote yourself to the prayers?
data.closing-prayer#Closing Prayer:
Merciful God, as a potter fashions a vessel from humble clay, you form us into a new creation. Shape us, day by day, through the cross of Christ your Son, until we pray as continually as we breathe and all our acts are prayer; through Jesus Christ and in the mystery of the Holy Spirit, we pray. Amen.
— Book of Common Worship, p.108
data.creed-and-heart#Creed and Heart:
Ask: Who remembers the Apostles’ Creed?
What is a creed? The word creed is connected to the words “to give your heart.” Say:
We want to look at prayer as an attitude of the heart. Your challenge tonight will be to make a commitment to prayer.
data.debrief#Debrief:
Ask:
Was it hard or easy to keep repeating the phrase? Why? How did it feel to offer your heart to God? How did it feel to imagine the Holy Spirit flowing into and through your heart? If you prayed this prayer several times a day — “Christ, be in my heart” — how do you think it might or might not change you?
data.devote--therefore#Devote — Therefore:
Say:
Heart — the dwelling place for our emotions. The heart is our center.
Devotion comes from the Latin devovere, meaning to consecrate. Devotion is a sacred commitment of the heart.
When we devote ourselves to something, we respond with a therefore — an action.
Examples from the first three Acts 2:42 vows:
I will devote myself to the church’s teaching, therefore I promise to… attend Sunday School, read and study the Bible, join a Bible-study group… I will devote myself to the fellowship, therefore I promise to… attend church, go on a youth retreat, attend youth group… I will devote myself to the breaking of bread, therefore I promise to… eat dinner with my family instead of in my room… I will devote myself to the prayers, therefore I promise to… (Write on the board.) Pray before bed every night / say grace at meals even in a restaurant / pray for wisdom or strength when I’m having a problem at school or with friends / pray for others… Have each confirmand come up with their own “therefore” for devoting themselves to prayer.
data.molding-hearts-activity-#Molding Hearts:
Give each student some clay and ask them to shape a three-dimensional heart (not flat).
While they’re molding, teachers read aloud:
Psalm 51:10, 17 — Create in me a pure heart, O God, and renew a steadfast spirit within me… My sacrifice, O God, is a broken spirit; a broken and contrite heart you, God, will not despise.
Romans 5:5 — And hope does not put us to shame, because God’s love has been poured out into our hearts through the Holy Spirit, who has been given to us.
When finished, give each person a bowl and a cup of water. Ask them to set their clay hearts in the bowl and pour in a small amount of water.
Ask:
What happens to the water? What would have to happen for the water to get inside the heart? Pour more water on the heart.
Ask:
Is there a connection between hearts and prayer? Why would God find broken hearts acceptable? How would prayer help lead us to broken hearts? Give everyone a paper towel and ask them to pat their hearts dry, leaving them in their original shapes.
data.opener--heart-words#Opener — Heart Words:
Ask everyone to come up with a word or phrase that has “heart” in it. Whoever comes up with one last loses — and has to face the consequences (don’t tell them what they are). No repeats. If you’d like, write them on the board.
Ask:
Why is the word heart so important in our language? (The person who “lost” goes first.) Why do people pray? Do you pray? Why or why not?
data.read--prayer-in-the-bible#Read — Prayer:
Ask volunteers to read:
Luke 18:1 — Then Jesus told his disciples a parable to show them that they should always pray and not lose heart.
Colossians 4:2 — Devote yourselves to prayer, being watchful and thankful.
1 Thessalonians 5:16–18 — Rejoice always, pray continually, give thanks in all circumstances; for this is God’s will for you in Christ Jesus.
Ask:
What does each passage say about prayer? What makes that hard to do? How could it be possible to do what the scripture teaches? (God will transform us to continually walk with and know him.) What does it mean “not to lose heart” in Luke 18:1? How might prayer keep us from losing heart?
data.silent-prayer#Silent Prayer:
Encourage students to sit comfortably with some space around them. Ask them to close their eyes and hold their clay hearts in their open palms.
Say:
Imagine this is your heart. It’s a little cracked and there may be small holes in it. As you hold it in your open palms, imagine offering it to God — not just once but over and over again. Minute after minute, you offer God your heart. Imagine the Holy Spirit flowing into the brokenness of your heart.
How does it feel to have the Spirit in your heart?
Say quietly to yourself: “Christ, be in my heart.” (Write the phrase on the board.)
Repeat the prayer. Allow other thoughts to flow away from you as you repeat, “Christ, be in my heart.”

data: Part B of Session 11 · Prayer.
data.appendix--situationscripture-strips#Appe: Print, cut, and distribute to the matching-game groups.
data.before-class#Before class: Confirmation ground rules Four sets of situation / scripture strips (see appendix) cut apart into two piles Optional: prayer-walk materials (for a park or sanctuary walk) Optional: printed finger labyrinths (one per student)
data.close--the-psalter#Close — The Psalter:
Sit in a circle. Have each person read one of the following passages from the book of Psalms. Psalms is the prayer book of the Bible — it has prayers for every occasion and emotion.
Psalm 9:1 Psalm 10:17–18 Psalm 13:5–6 Psalm 15:2 Psalm 16:7–8 Psalm 19:8–9 Psalm 19:14 Psalm 26:2–3 Psalm 27:14 Psalm 28:7 Pray:
Gracious God, you know our hearts. We pray that you will enter our hearts, seeping in through our brokenness. Teach us how to pray constantly, so that our lives might be a closer reflection of what you would have us to be in the world. Amen.
Close with the Lord’s Prayer.
data.discussion#Discussion:
Ask:
What have you learned today that has helped you better understand prayer as having an open heart to God? What are you still wondering? How does praying help you know you are God’s beloved child? How will you devote yourself to prayer?
data.opener--matching-prayer-with-scripture#O:
Break the group into 3–4 smaller groups. Give each group a set of situation strips and a set of scripture strips. Have them match the scriptures to the situations. (Appendix at the bottom of this page.)
Invite them to come back and go through them together.
Say:
When we don’t know what to pray, we can pray the words of Scripture.
We can also pray the Lord’s Prayer.
data.option-a--prayer-walk-in-the-park#Option:
Create a prayer walk in a nearby park incorporating:
Silence Scripture Repetition The five senses
data.option-b--acts-prayer#Option B — ACTS Pr:
Teach the ACTS framework:
Adoration Confession Thanksgiving Supplication Have students try and fill in prayer phrases for each.
data.option-c--finger-labyrinth#Option C — Fi:
Hand out printed finger labyrinths. Have each student turn around to face away from the group, or spread out.
Say:
For centuries, Christians have walked along the paths of labyrinths to focus their attention on praying and listening to God. Start by relaxing and taking a few deep breaths. As you move your finger along the labyrinth path, say one of the breath prayers by breathing in the first phrase and out the second:
Gracious God, / help me to love. Forgiving God, / have mercy on me. Holy God, / guide my actions. Lord Jesus, / help me to follow you. Say:
By practicing this type of prayer, you will become less distracted and more comfortable praying this way.
data.optional-deep-dives#Optional Deep Dives: Pick one (or more) of the following as time allows.
data.the-lords-prayer#The Lord\u0026rsquo;s P:
Pray together:
Our Father who art in heaven, hallowed be thy name. Thy kingdom come, thy will be done, on earth as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread, and forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors; and lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil. For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, forever. Amen.
Ask:
What challenges you about prayer? Do we have to say the right words when we pray? Do you think there’s anything off-limits? Anger? Sadness? Questioning? Say:
No. All prayer is music to God’s ears. The main point is that we commit to praying and seek to build a relationship with God. Though — like the Lord’s Prayer and the words of Scripture (especially the Psalms, the book of prayer in the Bible) — we have been given some awesome models of prayer.
data.the-scriptures#The scriptures: Psalm 51:10 — Create in me a clean heart, O God, and put a new and right spirit within me. 1 Corinthians 3:16 — Do you not know that you are God’s temple and that God’s Spirit dwells in you? 1 Peter 2:9 — But you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God’s own people, in order that you may proclaim the mighty acts of him who called you out of darkness into his marvelous light. Matthew 22:37, 39 — You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, and with all your soul, and with all your mind… You shall love your neighbor as yourself. Romans 8:38–39 — For I am convinced that neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor rulers, nor things present, nor things to come, nor powers, nor height, nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord.
data.the-situations-praying-for-one-another#T: In desperation, Sue cheated on a test. Now she feels miserably guilty. At a party, a friend is pressuring you to join in the drinking. The youth group is trying to decide what to do this summer. Within a week of moving to your school, Lee has been labeled a weirdo, a nerd, etc. Others are ignoring Lee. You can see the pain in his face. You got an F on your final exam. Your parents are fighting again. Your best friend is ignoring you to do things with others. You woke up with an enormous pimple on your chin.

data:
Session 12 · Crossing Over.
This is the final session of the Confirmation year. Parents are in the room. Looking back is a small part of tonight; looking ahead is the point.
data.1-personal--get-specific#1. Personal — g:
How will you build your personal relationship with God?
Prayer time Bible reading Devotional book Worship music
data.2-communal#2. Communal:
Where will you commit to gathering with the people of God?
Sunday School Youth group Church Christian friends and family
data.3-missional#3. Missional:
Where will you serve?
Mission trips Food pantry Children’s ministry Helping lead the youth ministry At your school Share in the big group.
data.before-class#Before class: Pens Notebooks Paper for the river-crossing game (enough “rocks” for each group) The Statement of Faith packet — one per confirmand
data.game--river-crossing#Game — River Crossi:
Break the group into 3–4 teams. Each team has a stretch of the room to cross and the same number of paper “rocks” as there are people on the team. Get across the river without touching the water (the floor).
Rules:
Can’t throw or slide the paper rocks If you touch water, you have to gather all your rocks and start over Each team gets 30 seconds to strategize Optional: parents vs. kids round
data.introducing-the-statement-of-faith#Intro:
Hand out the packet. See the Statement of Faith section of this site for the full framework, examples, and guides. The high-level points every student should touch on:
A little about yourself Your personal experience with God / how you experience God (worship, community, serving, art, writing, nature, a Scripture that speaks to you, etc.) Are there people or places that have impacted your faith? What you believe about God (think Apostles’ Creed) How you want to live out your faith An affirmation that you want to be a faithful member of the church, and how you want to contribute to its life The packet includes: sample statements from prior students, the Apostles’ Creed, statement-of-faith guides (for generating ideas), and the Big Four Questions. Students don’t have to include nearly everything on those guides — they’re prompts, not checklists.
data.look-ahead#Look Ahead:
Go over:
Confirmation Dessert — families welcome; just let us know Confirmation Sunday — what the service looks like, what’s expected of confirmands The Big Four Questions — what you’ll be asked in front of the congregation (see Session 1A) Calendar — remaining dates Pictures — need 3–4 pictures per confirmand spanning baby-to-now Statement of Faith — hand out the packet and walk through what it is
data.looking-ahead--personal--communal--missi: Hand out papers. Have students write their names at the top and work at their tables.
data.mentors#Mentors:
On the back of the paper:
Write down one or two adults in the church that you want to stay in touch with (not your parents). Ask them if they would be a mentor to you and continue to walk with you on your faith journey.
data.read--joshua-3717#Read — Joshua 3:7–17:
Introduce the reading:
In the Old Testament we follow the story of the Israelites — God’s people. They were enslaved in Egypt for hundreds of years. God worked through Moses to deliver them from Egypt. They were given the Ten Commandments. They built the tabernacle — the temporary mobile space where they worshipped God — and the Ark of the Covenant, where they kept their most sacred possessions. They wandered in the desert for 40 years. Eventually Moses died, and a new leader, Joshua, took his place.
We looked at Joshua at the beginning of the year to talk about the idea of a covenant — a sacred and secure promise for the sake of relationship.
Tonight our Scripture begins with Joshua and the Israelites standing on the Jordan River. The only thing between them and the Promised Land is the water.
Ask two volunteers to read: Joshua 3:7–13 and Joshua 3:14–17.
Joshua 3:14–17 — When the people set out from their tents to cross over the Jordan, the priests bearing the ark of the covenant were in front of the people. Now the Jordan overflows all its banks throughout the time of harvest. So when those who bore the ark had come to the Jordan and the feet of the priests bearing the ark were dipped in the edge of the water, the waters flowing from above stood still, rising up in a single heap far off at Adam, the city that is beside Zarethan, while those flowing toward the sea of the Arabah, the Dead Sea, were wholly cut off. Then the people crossed over opposite Jericho. While all Israel were crossing over on dry ground, the priests who bore the ark of the covenant of the Lord stood firmly on dry ground in the middle of the Jordan, until the entire nation finished crossing over the Jordan.
Ask:
Who do you think should get the credit for them crossing the river? (God, Joshua, the people, the priests.) — answer in big group Who are the “priests” that have helped you along in your Confirmation journey? Think both current and past. — talk at your table When the Israelites crossed over, was it the end of their journey or the beginning? (Close of one chapter and start of another.) — talk at your table Say:
God has brought you to this place. Some leaders have paved the way. You have put in the work. Your parents have walked with you. Your classmates have journeyed with you.
As you prepare to cross over, we want to think together about what lies ahead.
data.transition#Transition:
Say:

Draft · still being polished The credo project.
Every confirmand writes their own statement of faith during the final weeks of Confirmation and reads it aloud on Confirmation Sunday. It’s their own words, not their parents’, not the church’s — what they actually believe and why.
FrameworkThe Apostles’ Creed + the ‘Big 4’ questions + our writing guide draft ExamplesStatements from past classes to read, not to copy draft The examples are to prime the pump, not to template. The whole point of this project is that it’s their words. A student whose statement parrots a past example has missed it entirely — lovingly send them back to the drawing board.

Past students’ statements of faith.
Reading a prior year’s statements is the single fastest way to get a stuck confirmand unstuck. “Oh — it’s allowed to sound like that?” is the reaction you want.
Examples are to prime the pump, not to template. The whole point of this project is that it’s their words. A student whose statement parrots an example has missed the point entirely — lovingly send them back to the drawing board.
data.how-to-use-examples-in-class#How to use:
At the Session 12 workshop, or during the final parent class:
Read one aloud — the whole group listens. Ask: what did you notice? What made it land? Hand out 2–3 examples on paper and let students read them at their own pace. Ask each student to underline one line they wish they had written — then ask them, what would be the equivalent line, in their own life? That last step — the equivalent line — is where most first-draft statements get unstuck.
data.published-examples#Published examples: Holding space for examples. We will publish 2–3 past student statements here as examples for future confirmands — but only with the student’s (and parents’) written permission, and only in anonymized form (first name or initials, no identifiable details about other people). If you’re the maintainer: don’t publish statements without going back to the students and parents; most will say yes, some won’t, and that’s fine.
data.what-a-good-statement-tends-to-do#What a:
After reading a handful of past years, the common threads:
Starts specific. A memory, a doubt, a person who matters. Not “God has always been important to me.” Names the hard parts. The year that was dark, the prayer that felt unanswered, the doubt that hasn’t quite gone away. Real faith has weather. Lands in a specific commitment. Not “I will try to be a better person” — something like “I want to be the kid who sits with the new student at lunch,” or “I want to read a psalm before I look at my phone in the morning.” Sounds like the student. If you can’t hear their voice in it, they haven’t finished. Names the church by name. “I want to be a member of [Church Name]” is the point of the whole exercise.

data:
How to help a 9th grader write a statement of faith.
Three pieces of scaffolding we put in front of students during the last weeks of Confirmation. They aren’t a checklist — they’re a starter kit for students who’ve never had to write anything like this before.
data.1-the-apostles-creed#1. The Apostles\u00:
The Apostles’ Creed is the oldest, shortest, most widely-shared summary of Christian faith in the world. Every denomination that uses it says the same words. A student’s statement doesn’t have to quote the creed — but it should address what the creed addresses.
Use it as a checklist of territory. If a student’s statement skips one of these moves entirely, ask them why.
Who is God the Father? Maker of heaven and earth. Who is Jesus? His only Son, our Lord — conceived, born, suffered, crucified, died, buried, descended, rose, ascended, seated, coming back. Who is the Holy Spirit? The Spirit of God at work in the world. What is the church? The holy catholic (universal) church. The communion of saints. What does Christ offer? Forgiveness of sins. Resurrection of the body. Life everlasting.
data.2-the-big-4-questions#2. The Big 4 Quest:
A simpler framework. Each student writes a paragraph on each:
Who is God to you? Who is Jesus to you? Who is the Holy Spirit to you? What is the church to you — and what is your place in it? Some students will finish the Big 4 and realize they have a statement of faith. Others will use it as an outline to write a longer piece from.
data.3-the-writing-guide#3. The Writing Guide: A page-by-page walk through the drafting process. Use this as a worksheet the student fills in before they start prose.
data.editing-pass#Editing Pass:
Before the student reads aloud at the Confirmation Dessert, a teacher or mentor does a light editing pass:
Honesty check — is this actually what they believe, or what they think we want to hear? Voice check — does this sound like them, or like a Hallmark card? Apostles’ Creed check — have they at least touched God, Jesus, the Spirit, and the church? Length check — 2–3 minutes read aloud is ideal. Trim gently if it’s longer than 4 minutes. Do not rewrite for the student. An edited statement of faith is only worth what the student actually believes of it. Query, prompt, suggest — but let the words stay theirs.
data.page-1--who-am-i#Page 1 — Who am I?: What’s my name, grade, and a couple of things I care about? Who is important to me in this life (family, friends, a mentor)? What’s an experience of worship, nature, or service that has made me feel close to God?
data.page-2--who-is-god-to-me#Page 2 — Who is: How would I describe God to someone who had never heard the word? What has God done that I see in my own life? When has it been hard to trust God? What’s helped?
data.page-3--who-is-jesus-to-me#Page 3 — Who: Who is Jesus in the Bible — what do the gospels say about him? Who is Jesus for me — what has he done for me, and what am I learning to let him do? Which of the Big Four vows do I especially want to say yes to?
data.page-4--who-is-the-holy-spirit-to-me#Pag: How have I seen the Spirit at work — in me, in my family, in the church? What are the gifts I believe the Spirit has given me?
data.page-5--what-is-the-church-and-whats-my-: What do I love about the church? Where do I want to serve? What do I want my membership to do in the world?
data.page-6--my-confession#Page 6 — My confes: Write out a closing paragraph that begins “I believe …” and ends with "… and I want to be a faithful member of this church."

data:
Grades 6–8. The ages most adults pretend they forgot. The ages that, according to the Barna Group, are when nearly 64% of lifelong Christians first made a commitment to Christ. Coincidence? Probably not.
These lessons are written for the real middle schooler — the one who laughs too loud, then goes quiet when it matters, then asks a question that’ll make you rethink your whole theology on the car ride home.
data.series#Series: Sermon on the MountNine weeks in Matthew 5–7. A new way to be human.
data.what-to-expect-from-an-ms-lesson#What to:
Every lesson on this site follows the same template so volunteers aren’t left guessing:
Behind the Curtain — what the teacher needs to know before walking in Center Stage — the ONE thing you want students to walk out with Prop Department — the shopping list Directing the Scene — the actual plan: prayer, hook, Scripture, discussion, and a tangible “turn toward action” All MS lessons target ~45–60 minutes and are written assuming a group of 6–15 students, family-style. Scale the activities to your room.
